description: Rigorous normal-bidual support intersection and finite-cover upgrade
full_text:
Below is a corrected proof. I will prove the stronger identity

\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W},
\]

hence in particular \(q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}\). The stronger product formula is useful because the obstruction state in the upgrade step is not assumed normal on \(N\); mere finite-meet information is not enough for arbitrary states unless the relevant projections commute.

## 1. DKEP support preliminaries

Write \(H=L^2(M)\), and let

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}:B(H)\to N=(B(H)^\sharp_J)^*
\]

be the DKEP normal-bidual embedding. For an induced \(U\subset V\Gamma\), put \(I=U\cap W\), \(B_U=M_U\), and let \(e_U\) be the projection \(H\to L^2(M_U)\). DKEP define \(X_U=X_{M_U}\) as the hereditary \(C^*\)-algebra generated by \(xJyJ e_U\), \(x,y\in M\); then

\[
K_U^L=\overline{B(H)X_U}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,2}},\qquad
K_U=(K_U^L)^*\cap K_U^L,\qquad
K_U^{\infty,1}=\overline{K_U}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}=\overline{X_U}^{\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}}.
\]

Moreover \(K_U^{\infty,1}\) is the strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule generated by \(K_U\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Let \(q_U\in N\) be the identity/support projection of \((K_U^\sharp_J)^*\subset N\). The DKEP normal-bidual support calculus identifies \((K_U^\sharp_J)^*\) with the corner \(q_UNq_U\). Also, because \(M\) and \(JMJ\) act by multipliers on \(K_U\), \(q_U\) commutes with \(M\) and with \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

A key technical fact is that \(\iota(e_U)\) commutes with the normal support projection \(p_{\rm nor}\), so

\[
\varepsilon_U:=\iota_{\rm nor}(e_U)
\]

is an actual projection in \(N\). This is the same argument as DKEP/Ding–Elayavalli Lemma 3.8: vector states obtained after applying \(e_U\) remain normal on \(M\) and \(JMJ\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

Finally, we use the following approximate-unit description. Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra, and let \(\mathcal U_0\subset\mathcal A\) be a set of algebraic unitaries generating \(M\) ultraweakly. Let \(X_U^0\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(uJvJ e_U\), \(u,v\in\mathcal U_0\). Then \(X_U^0\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_U^{\infty,1}\). If \((h_\lambda^U)_\lambda\) is any positive contractive approximate unit of \(X_U^0\), chosen inside \(C^*(uJvJ e_U Jv^*Ju^*:u,v\in F_\lambda)\) for finite \(F_\lambda\subset\mathcal U_0\), then

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)\xrightarrow[\lambda]{\ \sigma\text{-weak}\ }q_U.
\]

This is precisely the DKEP approximate-unit argument: approximate units of a \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense subalgebra converge to the normal-bidual identity. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

## 2. Exact reduced-word finite propagation

For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), write

\[
L_aR_b:=aJb^*J,
\]

so \(L_aR_b(\widehat z)=\widehat{azb}\). The graph-product Wick reduction gives the following exact finite-propagation lemma.

**Lemma.** For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), there are finitely many \(\alpha_k,\beta_k,\gamma_k,\delta_k\in\mathcal A\) such that

\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
L_{\alpha_k}R_{\beta_k}\, e_I\, L_{\gamma_k}R_{\delta_k}e_W.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Equivalently, for every \(z\in\mathcal A\cap M_W\),

\[
E_U(azb)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
\alpha_k\,E_I(\gamma_k z\delta_k)\,\beta_k.
\tag{2.2}
\]

**Proof.** By linearity, reduce to \(a,b,z\) reduced graph-product words. Expand \(azb\) using the standard Wick reduction rules for graph products: letters are shuffled past commuting letters, equal-vertex adjacent letters are multiplied inside the corresponding vertex algebra, and each such vertex product is decomposed into its scalar plus trace-zero part.

Only finitely many letters from \(z\) can be involved in cancellations with the fixed words \(a\) and \(b\). If a letter of \(z\) has vertex in \(W\setminus U\), then for its contribution to survive under \(E_U\), that letter must be cancelled during the reduction. Thus every non-cancelled letter of \(z\) has vertex in \(I=U\cap W\). Hence each surviving reduction pattern has the form

\[
z \mapsto \alpha\,E_I(\gamma z\delta)\,\beta
\]

for fixed \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta\in\mathcal A\), and there are only finitely many possible cancellation/shuffle patterns because \(a\) and \(b\) are fixed finite words. Summing over those finitely many patterns gives (2.2), hence (2.1) on the dense subspace \(\mathcal A\cap M_W\), and therefore on \(L^2(M_W)\). ∎

The same formula with \(U,W\) interchanged also holds.

## 3. Finite triple propagation into \(K_I^{\infty,1}\)

Let \(Y_U^0\) be the \(*\)-algebra generated by the finite algebraic projections

\[
p_{u,v}^U:=uJvJ e_U Jv^*Ju^*,\qquad u,v\in\mathcal U_0.
\]

Thus \(X_U^0\) is the hereditary algebra generated by \(Y_U^0\). The crucial point is:

**Lemma.** If \(x\in Y_U^0\), \(y\in Y_W^0\), \(z\in Y_U^0\), then

\[
xyz\in X_I\subset K_I^{\infty,1}.
\tag{3.1}
\]

Consequently, for the positive approximate units \(h_\lambda^U\in X_U^0\), \(h_\mu^W\in X_W^0\),

\[
h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U\in K_I^{\infty,1}.
\tag{3.2}
\]

**Proof.** A monomial in \(Y_U^0Y_W^0Y_U^0\) is a finite product of algebraic \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-multipliers and projections \(e_U,e_W\), beginning and ending with an \(e_U\)-block and containing at least one \(e_W\)-block. Thus it is a finite linear combination of operators of the schematic form

\[
A_0 e_{S_1} A_1 e_{S_2}\cdots A_{m-1} e_{S_m}A_m,
\]

where each \(S_j\in\{U,W\}\), \(S_1=S_m=U\), at least one \(S_j=W\), and each \(A_j\) is a finite sum of algebraic \(L_aR_b\)’s.

Whenever a transition \(e_U A e_W\) or \(e_W A e_U\) occurs, apply (2.1). This replaces that transition by a finite sum of terms factoring through \(e_I\). Since \(I\subset U,W\), any subsequent adjacent \(e_U\)- or \(e_W\)-block next to an \(e_I\)-block is again reduced by the same finite-propagation formula with \(I\) in place of one of the two sets. Iterating through the finite monomial expresses it as a finite sum of terms

\[
L_\alpha R_\beta\, T\, L_\gamma R_\delta,
\qquad
T\in e_I B(H)e_I,
\]

which are, by definition, in \(X_I\). This proves (3.1). Functional calculus approximating \(h_\lambda^U,h_\mu^W\) by polynomials with zero constant term gives (3.2), because \(K_I^{\infty,1}\) is closed for \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\) and hence also for norm-convergent approximations. ∎

This is the step that repairs the false shortcut \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\). The twofold product is false because \(X_U\) contains large corners. The valid statement is the **triple** support statement: \(U\)-piece, then \(W\)-piece, then \(U\)-piece is forced through \(I=U\cap W\).

## 4. Support product formula

Because each \(h_\lambda^U\) is built from \(e_U\) and \(M,JMJ\)-multipliers, and \(\iota(e_U)\) commutes with \(p_{\rm nor}\), \(\iota_{\rm nor}\) is multiplicative on the finite \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the relevant \(h_\lambda^U,h_\mu^W\). Therefore (3.2) gives

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\mu^W)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)
=
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U)
\in q_I N q_I.
\]

The left side is a positive contraction. Hence

\[
0\le
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\mu^W)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)
\le q_I.
\]

Taking \(\sigma\)-weak limits first in \(\mu\) and then in \(\lambda\), using normality of multiplication in \(N\), gives

\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\tag{4.1}
\]

On the other hand, \(I\subset U,W\), so \(X_I\subset X_U\cap X_W\), hence \(K_I\subset K_U\cap K_W\), and therefore

\[
q_I\le q_U,\qquad q_I\le q_W.
\tag{4.2}
\]

Let \(p=q_U\), \(q=q_W\), \(r=q_I\). From \(r\le p,q\) and \(pqp\le r\),

\[
pqp=r+p(q-r)p\le r.
\]

Thus \(p(q-r)p=0\), hence \((q-r)p=0\). Therefore \(qp=rp=r\), and by taking adjoints \(pq=r\). Thus

\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\tag{4.3}
\]

In particular,

\[
q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}.
\]

Since the projections commute and their product is the intersection projection, induction gives the finite formula

\[
q_{U_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge q_{U_n}
=
q_{U_1}\cdots q_{U_n}
=
q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n}.
\tag{4.4}
\]

## 5. The empty set

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The hereditary algebra generated by \(xJyJ e_\varnothing\), \(x,y\in M\), contains the rank-one operators

\[
\theta_{\widehat x,\widehat y}
=
x e_\varnothing Jy^*J,
\]

and these span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(H)\). Hence

\[
X_\varnothing=K(H),\qquad K_\varnothing=K(H),\qquad q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{5.1}
\]

## 6. Upgrade from relative link proper proximality

Assume \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal. By the DKEP normal-bidual obstruction criterion, there is an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute compactification \(\widetilde S(M)\subset N\), normal on \(M\), such that

\[
\Omega|_M=\tau,\qquad
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{6.1}
\]

The vanishing on \(q_KNq_K=(K(H)^\sharp_J)^*\) is obtained by excluding the weakly compact/hypertrace alternative using the assumption that there is no amenable summand. This is the normal-bidual form of DKEP Lemma 8.6. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Now suppose \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\). I claim that every absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\) as above satisfies

\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\tag{6.2}
\]

Indeed, if \(r=1-q_U\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in\widetilde S_U(M)\)

\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]

Because \(q_U\) commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\), for \(a\in JMJ\),

\[
[rTr,a]=r[T,a]r=0,
\]

so \(rTr\in\widetilde S(M)\). Thus \(\Phi\) is well-defined. It is \(M\)-central because \(\Omega\) is \(M\)-central and \(r\) commutes with \(M\). Moreover \(\Phi|_M\) is normal: for \(x\in M_+\),

\[
0\le \Phi(x)=\frac{\Omega(rxr)}{\Omega(r)}
\le
\frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\frac{\tau(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]

Hence \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_U(M)\), normal on \(M\), contradicting proper proximality relative to \(X_U\). Therefore \(\Omega(q_U)=1\).

Apply this with \(U=\operatorname{Link}(v)\) for every vertex \(v\). Then

\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V\Gamma.
\]

By the product formula (4.4),

\[
\prod_{v\in V\Gamma} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_{\bigcap_{v\in V\Gamma}\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]

Because the graph has no loops, no vertex belongs to its own link, so

\[
\bigcap_{v\in V\Gamma}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]

Thus

\[
\prod_{v\in V\Gamma} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_\varnothing
=
q_K.
\]

The projections commute, and a state that is \(1\) on each commuting projection is \(1\) on their product. Hence \(\Omega(q_K)=1\), contradicting (6.1). Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.

## 7. What was wrong in the t2 draft

Two points needed repair.

1. The inclusion \(X_UX_W\subset X_{U\cap W}\) is false because \(X_U\) contains the large corner \(e_UB(H)e_U\). The correct statement is a triple support statement: finite \(U\)-approximate-unit pieces, then \(W\)-pieces, then \(U\)-pieces are forced through \(U\cap W\) after passing to \(K_{U\cap W}^{\infty,1}\).

2. For the upgrade step, the obstruction state \(\Omega\) is not normal on all of \(N\). Thus one cannot use only \(q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}\) unless one also knows commutation/product. The repaired proof proves the stronger formula \(q_Uq_W=q_{U\cap W}\), so the finite product argument is legitimate for arbitrary states.

\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W},

## 2. Exact reduced-word finite propagation

For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), write

\[
L_aR_b:=aJb^*J,

**Lemma.** For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), there are finitely many \(\alpha_k,\beta_k,\gamma_k,\delta_k\in\mathcal A\) such that

\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
L_{\alpha_k}R_{\beta_k}\, e_I\, L_{\gamma_k}R_{\delta_k}e_W.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Equivalently, for every \(z\in\mathcal A\cap M_W\),

\[
E_U(azb)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
\alpha_k\,E_I(\gamma_k z\delta_k)\,\beta_k.
\tag{2.2}

for fixed \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta\in\mathcal A\), and there are only finitely many possible cancellation/shuffle patterns because \(a\) and \(b\) are fixed finite words. ## 3. Finite triple propagation into \(K_I^{\infty,1}\)

Let \(Y_U^0\) be the \(*\)-algebra generated by the finite algebraic projections

\[
p_{u,v}^U:=uJvJ e_U Jv^*Ju^*,\qquad u,v\in\mathcal U_0.
\]

Thus \(X_U^0\) is the hereditary algebra generated by \(Y_U^0\). The crucial point is:

**Lemma.** If \(x\in Y_U^0\), \(y\in Y_W^0\), \(z\in Y_U^0\), then

\[
xyz\in X_I\subset K_I^{\infty,1}.
\tag{3.1}
\]

Consequently, for the positive approximate units \(h_\lambda^U\in X_U^0\), \(h_\mu^W\in X_W^0\),

\[
h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U\in K_I^{\infty,1}.
\tag{3.2}
\]

**Proof.** A monomial in \(Y_U^0Y_W^0Y_U^0\) is a finite product of algebraic \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-multipliers and projections \(e_U,e_W\), beginning and ending with an \(e_U\)-block and containing at least one \(e_W\)-block. Thus it is a finite linear combination of operators of the schematic form

\[
A_0 e_{S_1} A_1 e_{S_2}\cdots A_{m-1} e_{S_m}A_m,
\]

where each \(S_j\in\{U,W\}\), \(S_1=S_m=U\), at least one \(S_j=W\), and each \(A_j\) is a finite sum of algebraic \(L_aR_b\)’s.

Whenever a transition \(e_U A e_W\) or \(e_W A e_U\) occurs, apply (2.1). This replaces that transition by a finite sum of terms factoring through \(e_I\). Since \(I\subset U,W\), any subsequent adjacent \(e_U\)- or \(e_W\)-block next to an \(e_I\)-block is again reduced by the same finite-propagation formula with \(I\) in place of one of the two sets. Iterating through the finite monomial expresses it as a finite sum of terms

\[
L_\alpha R_\beta\, T\, L_\gamma R_\delta,
\qquad
T\in e_I B(H)e_I,
\]

which are, by definition, in \(X_I\). This proves (3.1). Functional calculus approximating \(h_\lambda^U,h_\mu^W\) by polynomials with zero constant term gives (3.2), because \(K_I^{\infty,1}\) is closed for \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\) and hence also for norm-convergent approximations. ∎

This is the step that repairs the false shortcut \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\). The twofold product is false because \(X_U\) contains large corners. The valid statement is the **triple** support statement: \(U\)-piece, then \(W\)-piece, then \(U\)-piece is forced through \(I=U\cap W\).

## 4. Support product formula

Because each \(h_\lambda^U\) is built from \(e_U\) and \(M,JMJ\)-multipliers, and \(\iota(e_U)\) commutes with \(p_{\rm nor}\), \(\iota_{\rm nor}\) is multiplicative on the finite \(C^*\)-algebra generated by the relevant \(h_\lambda^U,h_\mu^W\). Therefore (3.2) gives

\[
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\mu^W)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)
=
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U h_\mu^W h_\lambda^U)
\in q_I N q_I.
\]

The left side is a positive contraction. Hence

\[
0\le
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\mu^W)\,
\iota_{\rm nor}(h_\lambda^U)
\le q_I.
\]

Taking \(\sigma\)-weak limits first in \(\mu\) and then in \(\lambda\), using normality of multiplication in \(N\), gives

\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\tag{4.1}
\]

On the other hand, \(I\subset U,W\), so \(X_I\subset X_U\cap X_W\), hence \(K_I\subset K_U\cap K_W\), and therefore

\[
q_I\le q_U,\qquad q_I\le q_W.
\tag{4.2}
\]

Let \(p=q_U\), \(q=q_W\), \(r=q_I\). From \(r\le p,q\) and \(pqp\le r\),

\[
pqp=r+p(q-r)p\le r.
\]

Thus \(p(q-r)p=0\), hence \((q-r)p=0\). Therefore \(qp=rp=r\), and by taking adjoints \(pq=r\). Thus

\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\tag{4.3}
\]

In particular,

\[
q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}.
\]

Since the projections commute and their product is the intersection projection, induction gives the finite formula

\[
q_{U_1}\wedge\cdots\wedge q_{U_n}
=
q_{U_1}\cdots q_{U_n}
=
q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n}.
\tag{4.4}
\]

## 5. The empty set

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The hereditary algebra generated by \(xJyJ e_\varnothing\), \(x,y\in M\), contains the rank-one operators

\[
\theta_{\widehat x,\widehat y}
=
x e_\varnothing Jy^*J,
\]

and these span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(H)\). Hence

\[
X_\varnothing=K(H),\qquad K_\varnothing=K(H),\qquad q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{5.1}
\]

## 6. Upgrade from relative link proper proximality

Assume \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal. By the DKEP normal-bidual obstruction criterion, there is an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute compactification \(\widetilde S(M)\subset N\), normal on \(M\), such that

\[
\Omega|_M=\tau,\qquad
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{6.1}
\]

The vanishing on \(q_KNq_K=(K(H)^\sharp_J)^*\) is obtained by excluding the weakly compact/hypertrace alternative using the assumption that there is no amenable summand. This is the normal-bidual form of DKEP Lemma 8.6. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

Now suppose \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\). I claim that every absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\) as above satisfies

\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\tag{6.2}
\]

Indeed, if \(r=1-q_U\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in\widetilde S_U(M)\)

\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]

Because \(q_U\) commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\), for \(a\in JMJ\),

\[
[rTr,a]=r[T,a]r=0,
\]

so \(rTr\in\widetilde S(M)\). Thus \(\Phi\) is well-defined. It is \(M\)-central because \(\Omega\) is \(M\)-central and \(r\) commutes with \(M\). Moreover \(\Phi|_M\) is normal: for \(x\in M_+\),

\[
0\le \Phi(x)=\frac{\Omega(rxr)}{\Omega(r)}
\le
\frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\frac{\tau(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]

Hence \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_U(M)\), normal on \(M\), contradicting proper proximality relative to \(X_U\). Therefore \(\Omega(q_U)=1\).

Apply this with \(U=\operatorname{Link}(v)\) for every vertex \(v\). Then

\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V\Gamma.
\]

By the product formula (4.4),

\[
\prod_{v\in V\Gamma} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_{\bigcap_{v\in V\Gamma}\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]

Because the graph has no loops, no vertex belongs to its own link, so

\[
\bigcap_{v\in V\Gamma}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]

Thus

\[
\prod_{v\in V\Gamma} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_\varnothing
=
q_K.
\]

The projections commute, and a state that is \(1\) on each commuting projection is \(1\) on their product. Hence \(\Omega(q_K)=1\), contradicting (6.1). Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.

## 7. What was wrong in the t2 draft

Two points needed repair.

1. The inclusion \(X_UX_W\subset X_{U\cap W}\) is false because \(X_U\) contains the large corner \(e_UB(H)e_U\). The correct statement is a triple support statement: finite \(U\)-approximate-unit pieces, then \(W\)-pieces, then \(U\)-pieces are forced through \(U\cap W\) after passing to \(K_{U\cap W}^{\infty,1}\).

2. For the upgrade step, the obstruction state \(\Omega\) is not normal on all of \(N\). Thus one cannot use only \(q_U\wedge q_W=q_{U\cap W}\) unless one also knows commutation/product. The repaired proof proves the stronger formula \(q_Uq_W=q_{U\cap W}\), so the finite product argument is legitimate for arbitrary states.

self_declared_problem_solved:
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

description: Assemble and red-team all non-support parts of the final proof
full_text:
Below is a complete proof skeleton, with the **support-product lemma**
\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}
\]
taken as the allowed black box. I also flag the hidden assumptions needed for the DKEP normal-bidual formulation.

---

## 0. Conventions and external DKEP inputs

Interpret “irreducible” in the standard graph-product sense: there is **no nontrivial join decomposition**
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_i\neq\varnothing,
\]
such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). If empty subgraphs were allowed in the definition, no graph would satisfy the condition, so the nonempty/nontrivial convention is necessary.

For \(U\subset V\), write \(M_U\) for the graph-product subalgebra, \(e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)\) for the Jones projection, \(X_U\) for the DKEP boundary piece generated by \(e_U\), and \(q_U\) for its normal-bidual support projection.

We use these standard DKEP facts:

1. \(S_{X_U}(M)\) consists of operators whose commutators with the right \(M\)-action are \(X_U\)-compact; the generator criterion is valid.
2. Relative proper proximality to \(X_U\) means: no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.
3. If \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, there is an absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\) in the normal-bidual compactification, normal on \(M\), with
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0,
   \]
   where \(q_K\) is the compact support.
4. The support projections \(q_U\) commute with the left and right \(M\)-actions.
5. **Allowed black box:** for all \(U,W\subset V\),
   \[
   q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}.
   \]

---

## 1. Graph-combinatorial consequences of irreducibility

Fix \(v\in V\). Let
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad \operatorname{Star}(v)=L\cup\{v\},\qquad N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v).
\]

### Star is not all of \(V\)

If \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every other vertex, so
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
is a nontrivial join decomposition. Since \(|V|\ge 3\), both sides are nonempty. Thus irreducibility forces
\[
N(v)\neq\varnothing.
\]

### Unique non-neighbor case

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L.
\]
Because \(|V|\ge 3\), \(L\neq\varnothing\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then every vertex in \(\{v,w\}\) would be adjacent to every vertex in \(L\), giving a join decomposition
\[
\Gamma=\Gamma_{\{v,w\}}*\Gamma_L,
\]
contradiction. Hence in the unique non-neighbor case there exists
\[
z\in L
\]
such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\).

These are the only graph-combinatorial facts needed later.

---

## 2. AFP decomposition

Set
\[
B=M_L,\qquad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
and the expectation \(E_B:A\to B\) is
\[
E_B=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

The standard graph-product normal form gives
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]
Indeed, \(A\) is generated by \(B\) and \(M_v\), while \(C\) is generated by \(B\) and the vertex algebras \(M_w\) for \(w\neq v\). Since no vertex in \(V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\) is adjacent to \(v\), alternating \(B\)-centered words from \(A\ominus B\) and \(C\ominus B\) remain orthogonal to \(L^2(B)\). Thus the \(B\)-valued mixed moments vanish, which is precisely amalgamated freeness over \(B\).

---

## 3. Sector projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly, and put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

Let \(\rho(x)=Jx^*J\) denote right multiplication. For \(D\in\{A,C\}\), \(x\in D^\circ:=\ker E_B|_D\), right multiplication by \(x\) only changes the last AFP letter. Hence it does not change whether the first letter is \(D\), except for the harmless \(L^2(B)\)-boundary already included in \(R_D\). Therefore
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)]
=e_B\rho(x)-\rho(x)e_B.
\]
Both terms factor through \(L^2(B)\), hence are \(X_L\)-compact. For \(b\in B\), right multiplication by \(b\) preserves every AFP sector, so the commutators vanish.

Every vertex algebra lies in \(A\) or \(C\), and these vertex algebras generate \(M\). Thus the DKEP generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in X_L\subset S_{X_L}(M)\), also
\[
P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

No hidden \(e_B\)-error appears here: the only sector changes caused by right multiplication are exactly the two terms \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\), both \(X_L\)-compact.

---

## 4. Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\). Since
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
put
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1_B=0.
\]

Now construct \(c_1,c_2\in C\).

### Case 1: at least two non-neighbors

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\). Let \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\) be trace-zero unitaries and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]
For \(i\neq j\), the product \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) has support outside \(L\), so \(E_B\) kills it. This remains true whether or not \(w_1,w_2\) are adjacent.

### Case 2: unique non-neighbor

Let \(N(v)=\{w\}\). By the graph argument above, choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries
\[
u_w\in M_w,\qquad u_z\in M_z\subset B,
\]
and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries, and
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). For the cross term,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) and \(w\) are not adjacent, the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced; the two \(w\)-letters cannot be shuffled together. Hence the word contains a non-link vertex and is killed by \(E_B\):
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\).

This also covers order-two trace-zero unitaries, e.g. \(M_w\simeq\mathbb C^2\). Even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), the intervening noncommuting \(u_z\) prevents cancellation.

Thus in all cases we have unitaries
\[
a\in A,\qquad c_1,c_2\in C
\]
such that
\[
E_B(a)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

---

## 5. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M),\qquad H_C^+=R_C L^2(M).
\]

If \(d\in C\) satisfies \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\). Indeed:

- \(dL^2(B)\subset L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B)\);
- if a reduced AFP word begins with an \(A\)-letter, then multiplying on the left by \(d\in C^\circ\) produces a reduced word beginning with \(C\).

Hence
\[
dH_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M).
\]
Applying this to \(d=c_i\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

The two ranges are orthogonal. Since
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0,
\]
we also have
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M).
\]
But
\[
P_C L^2(M)\perp H_A^+.
\]
Thus
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*\perp c_2R_Ac_2^*,
\]
and therefore
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A\) and \(E_B(a)=0\),
\[
aH_C^+\subset P_A L^2(M),
\]
so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

Again, there is no \(e_B\)-error: centered \(A\)- or \(C\)-letters send \(L^2(B)\) into \(A\ominus B\) or \(C\ominus B\), not back into \(L^2(B)\).

---

## 6. Relative central-state contradiction

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\varphi(uTu^*)=\varphi(T)
\]
for \(u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}\).

From
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C,
\]
we get
\[
2\varphi(R_A)\le \varphi(P_C).
\]
From
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A,
\]
we get
\[
\varphi(R_C)\le \varphi(P_A).
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\varphi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\varphi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\varphi(P_C).
\]
Then
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1,
\]
and
\[
\varphi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \varphi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Hence \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). In particular, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every vertex \(v\).

---

## 7. No amenable direct summand

This step is important because one cannot prove nonamenability merely by taking two nonadjacent trace-zero unitaries: if both vertex algebras are \(\mathbb C^2\), two order-two unitaries generate \(L(\mathbb Z_2*\mathbb Z_2)\), which is amenable.

Instead, use the relative ping-pong result.

Suppose \(z\in Z(M)\) is nonzero and \(zM\) is amenable. Let \(H_z=zL^2(M)\cong L^2(zM)\). Amenability of \(zM\) gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(H_z)\). For fixed \(v\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{H_z}),\qquad T\in S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)=\psi((zx)(zTz))
=\psi((zTz)(zx))
=\Phi(Tx),
\]
because \(zx\in zM\) and \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central. Thus \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\), contradicting Step 6.

Therefore \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

## 8. Absolute obstruction state and compression

Assume for contradiction that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, the DKEP obstruction theorem gives an absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

Now fix \(U\subset V\), and suppose \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\). We claim
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\]

Let
\[
r=1-q_U.
\]
If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in S_{X_U}(M)\)
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
This is well-defined in the normal-bidual formulation because \(q_U\) supports the \(X_U\)-compact ideal. Since \(r\) commutes with the left and right \(M\)-actions,
\[
[rTr,\rho(x)]
=
r[T,\rho(x)]r
=
0
\]
for \(x\in M\). Hence \(rTr\) belongs to the absolute compactification on which \(\Omega\) is defined.

The functional \(\Phi\) is a state. It is \(M\)-central because \(r\) commutes with \(M\) and \(\Omega\) is \(M\)-central:
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\frac{\Omega(x\,rTr)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\frac{\Omega(rTr\,x)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\Phi(Tx).
\]
Moreover, for \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le \Phi(x)
=
\frac{\Omega(rxr)}{\Omega(r)}
\le
\frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
Since \(\Omega|_M\) is normal, \(\Phi|_M\) is normal. This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X_U\). Therefore
\[
\Omega(1-q_U)=0,
\]
so
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\]

Applying this to \(U=\operatorname{Link}(v)\) for every \(v\in V\), Step 6 gives
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V.
\]

Hidden assumption repaired here: \(\Omega\) must be taken in the DKEP normal-bidual setting, not merely as an abstract state on a smaller \(C^*\)-algebra, so that the projections \(q_U\) and the compressions \(rTr\) are in its domain.

---

## 9. Final finite product contradiction

By the allowed support-product lemma,
\[
q_Uq_W=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Thus the projections \(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) commute, and
\[
\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]

Because the graph is simplicial and loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_\varnothing.
\]

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull of \(e_\varnothing\) contains the finite-rank operators
\[
x e_\varnothing J y^*J,
\]
whose span is norm-dense in \(K(L^2(M))\). Hence
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

Since \(\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1\) for every \(v\), and these projections commute, positivity gives
\[
\Omega\left(\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Indeed,
\[
1-\prod_v q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\le
\sum_v \left(1-q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right),
\]
so applying \(\Omega\) gives zero on the left.

Thus
\[
\Omega(q_K)=1,
\]
contradicting the obstruction-state property
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.

---

## Hidden assumptions and repairs

1. **Irreducibility must mean no nontrivial join.**  
   Empty join pieces must be excluded.

2. **The graph product normal form is required.**  
   The AFP decomposition relies on the standard reduced-word theorem for graph products.

3. **The DKEP normal-bidual formulation is required.**  
   The compression argument needs \(\Omega\) to be defined on the support projections \(q_U\) and on \(rTr\).

4. **The support-product lemma must give products, not only meets.**  
   Since \(\Omega\) need not be normal on the whole normal bidual, knowing only a meet formula is insufficient. The commuting product formula fixes this.

5. **No free-Haar or diffuseness assumption is available.**  
   The proof only uses trace-zero unitaries and works for finite-dimensional vertices, including order-two unitaries.

With these conventions and the allowed support-product lemma, the proof is complete.

solution:
Below is a complete proof skeleton, with the **support-product lemma**
\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}
\]
taken as the allowed black box. I also flag the hidden assumptions needed for the DKEP normal-bidual formulation.

---

## 0. Conventions and external DKEP inputs

Interpret “irreducible” in the standard graph-product sense: there is **no nontrivial join decomposition**
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_i\neq\varnothing,
\]
such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). If empty subgraphs were allowed in the definition, no graph would satisfy the condition, so the nonempty/nontrivial convention is necessary.

For \(U\subset V\), write \(M_U\) for the graph-product subalgebra, \(e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)\) for the Jones projection, \(X_U\) for the DKEP boundary piece generated by \(e_U\), and \(q_U\) for its normal-bidual support projection.

We use these standard DKEP facts:

1. \(S_{X_U}(M)\) consists of operators whose commutators with the right \(M\)-action are \(X_U\)-compact; the generator criterion is valid.
2. Relative proper proximality to \(X_U\) means: no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_U}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.
3. If \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, there is an absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\) in the normal-bidual compactification, normal on \(M\), with
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0,
   \]
   where \(q_K\) is the compact support.
4. The support projections \(q_U\) commute with the left and right \(M\)-actions.
5. **Allowed black box:** for all \(U,W\subset V\),
   \[
   q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}.
   \]

---

## 1. Graph-combinatorial consequences of irreducibility

Fix \(v\in V\). Let
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad \operatorname{Star}(v)=L\cup\{v\},\qquad N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v).
\]

### Star is not all of \(V\)

If \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every other vertex, so
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
is a nontrivial join decomposition. Since \(|V|\ge 3\), both sides are nonempty. Thus irreducibility forces
\[
N(v)\neq\varnothing.
\]

### Unique non-neighbor case

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L.
\]
Because \(|V|\ge 3\), \(L\neq\varnothing\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then every vertex in \(\{v,w\}\) would be adjacent to every vertex in \(L\), giving a join decomposition
\[
\Gamma=\Gamma_{\{v,w\}}*\Gamma_L,
\]
contradiction. Hence in the unique non-neighbor case there exists
\[
z\in L
\]
such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\).

These are the only graph-combinatorial facts needed later.

---

## 2. AFP decomposition

Set
\[
B=M_L,\qquad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
and the expectation \(E_B:A\to B\) is
\[
E_B=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

The standard graph-product normal form gives
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]
Indeed, \(A\) is generated by \(B\) and \(M_v\), while \(C\) is generated by \(B\) and the vertex algebras \(M_w\) for \(w\neq v\). Since no vertex in \(V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\) is adjacent to \(v\), alternating \(B\)-centered words from \(A\ominus B\) and \(C\ominus B\) remain orthogonal to \(L^2(B)\). Thus the \(B\)-valued mixed moments vanish, which is precisely amalgamated freeness over \(B\).

---

## 3. Sector projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly, and put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

Let \(\rho(x)=Jx^*J\) denote right multiplication. For \(D\in\{A,C\}\), \(x\in D^\circ:=\ker E_B|_D\), right multiplication by \(x\) only changes the last AFP letter. Hence it does not change whether the first letter is \(D\), except for the harmless \(L^2(B)\)-boundary already included in \(R_D\). Therefore
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)]
=e_B\rho(x)-\rho(x)e_B.
\]
Both terms factor through \(L^2(B)\), hence are \(X_L\)-compact. For \(b\in B\), right multiplication by \(b\) preserves every AFP sector, so the commutators vanish.

Every vertex algebra lies in \(A\) or \(C\), and these vertex algebras generate \(M\). Thus the DKEP generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in X_L\subset S_{X_L}(M)\), also
\[
P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

No hidden \(e_B\)-error appears here: the only sector changes caused by right multiplication are exactly the two terms \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\), both \(X_L\)-compact.

---

## 4. Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\). Since
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
put
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1_B=0.
\]

Now construct \(c_1,c_2\in C\).

### Case 1: at least two non-neighbors

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\). Let \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\) be trace-zero unitaries and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]
For \(i\neq j\), the product \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) has support outside \(L\), so \(E_B\) kills it. This remains true whether or not \(w_1,w_2\) are adjacent.

### Case 2: unique non-neighbor

Let \(N(v)=\{w\}\). By the graph argument above, choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries
\[
u_w\in M_w,\qquad u_z\in M_z\subset B,
\]
and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries, and
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). For the cross term,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) and \(w\) are not adjacent, the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced; the two \(w\)-letters cannot be shuffled together. Hence the word contains a non-link vertex and is killed by \(E_B\):
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\).

This also covers order-two trace-zero unitaries, e.g. \(M_w\simeq\mathbb C^2\). Even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), the intervening noncommuting \(u_z\) prevents cancellation.

Thus in all cases we have unitaries
\[
a\in A,\qquad c_1,c_2\in C
\]
such that
\[
E_B(a)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

---

## 5. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_A L^2(M),\qquad H_C^+=R_C L^2(M).
\]

If \(d\in C\) satisfies \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_C L^2(M)\). Indeed:

- \(dL^2(B)\subset L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B)\);
- if a reduced AFP word begins with an \(A\)-letter, then multiplying on the left by \(d\in C^\circ\) produces a reduced word beginning with \(C\).

Hence
\[
dH_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M).
\]
Applying this to \(d=c_i\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

The two ranges are orthogonal. Since
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0,
\]
we also have
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_C L^2(M).
\]
But
\[
P_C L^2(M)\perp H_A^+.
\]
Thus
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*\perp c_2R_Ac_2^*,
\]
and therefore
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A\) and \(E_B(a)=0\),
\[
aH_C^+\subset P_A L^2(M),
\]
so
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

Again, there is no \(e_B\)-error: centered \(A\)- or \(C\)-letters send \(L^2(B)\) into \(A\ominus B\) or \(C\ominus B\), not back into \(L^2(B)\).

---

## 6. Relative central-state contradiction

Let \(\varphi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\varphi(uTu^*)=\varphi(T)
\]
for \(u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}\).

From
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C,
\]
we get
\[
2\varphi(R_A)\le \varphi(P_C).
\]
From
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A,
\]
we get
\[
\varphi(R_C)\le \varphi(P_A).
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\varphi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\varphi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\varphi(P_C).
\]
Then
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1,
\]
and
\[
\varphi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \varphi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,\qquad \alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta,
\]
so
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Hence \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). In particular, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) for every vertex \(v\).

---

## 7. No amenable direct summand

This step is important because one cannot prove nonamenability merely by taking two nonadjacent trace-zero unitaries: if both vertex algebras are \(\mathbb C^2\), two order-two unitaries generate \(L(\mathbb Z_2*\mathbb Z_2)\), which is amenable.

Instead, use the relative ping-pong result.

Suppose \(z\in Z(M)\) is nonzero and \(zM\) is amenable. Let \(H_z=zL^2(M)\cong L^2(zM)\). Amenability of \(zM\) gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(H_z)\). For fixed \(v\), define
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(zTz|_{H_z}),\qquad T\in S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)=\psi((zx)(zTz))
=\psi((zTz)(zx))
=\Phi(Tx),
\]
because \(zx\in zM\) and \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central. Thus \(\Phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}(M)\), contradicting Step 6.

Therefore \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

## 8. Absolute obstruction state and compression

Assume for contradiction that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, the DKEP obstruction theorem gives an absolute obstruction state \(\Omega\), normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

Now fix \(U\subset V\), and suppose \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_U\). We claim
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\]

Let
\[
r=1-q_U.
\]
If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in S_{X_U}(M)\)
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
This is well-defined in the normal-bidual formulation because \(q_U\) supports the \(X_U\)-compact ideal. Since \(r\) commutes with the left and right \(M\)-actions,
\[
[rTr,\rho(x)]
=
r[T,\rho(x)]r
=
0
\]
for \(x\in M\). Hence \(rTr\) belongs to the absolute compactification on which \(\Omega\) is defined.

The functional \(\Phi\) is a state. It is \(M\)-central because \(r\) commutes with \(M\) and \(\Omega\) is \(M\)-central:
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\frac{\Omega(x\,rTr)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\frac{\Omega(rTr\,x)}{\Omega(r)}
=
\Phi(Tx).
\]
Moreover, for \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le \Phi(x)
=
\frac{\Omega(rxr)}{\Omega(r)}
\le
\frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
Since \(\Omega|_M\) is normal, \(\Phi|_M\) is normal. This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X_U\). Therefore
\[
\Omega(1-q_U)=0,
\]
so
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\]

Applying this to \(U=\operatorname{Link}(v)\) for every \(v\in V\), Step 6 gives
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V.
\]

Hidden assumption repaired here: \(\Omega\) must be taken in the DKEP normal-bidual setting, not merely as an abstract state on a smaller \(C^*\)-algebra, so that the projections \(q_U\) and the compressions \(rTr\) are in its domain.

---

## 9. Final finite product contradiction

By the allowed support-product lemma,
\[
q_Uq_W=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Thus the projections \(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) commute, and
\[
\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]

Because the graph is simplicial and loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_\varnothing.
\]

For \(U=\varnothing\), \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). The hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\) hull of \(e_\varnothing\) contains the finite-rank operators
\[
x e_\varnothing J y^*J,
\]
whose span is norm-dense in \(K(L^2(M))\). Hence
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

Since \(\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1\) for every \(v\), and these projections commute, positivity gives
\[
\Omega\left(\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Indeed,
\[
1-\prod_v q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\le
\sum_v \left(1-q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right),
\]
so applying \(\Omega\) gives zero on the left.

Thus
\[
\Omega(q_K)=1,
\]
contradicting the obstruction-state property
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.

---

## Hidden assumptions and repairs

1. **Irreducibility must mean no nontrivial join.**  
   Empty join pieces must be excluded.

2. **The graph product normal form is required.**  
   The AFP decomposition relies on the standard reduced-word theorem for graph products.

3. **The DKEP normal-bidual formulation is required.**  
   The compression argument needs \(\Omega\) to be defined on the support projections \(q_U\) and on \(rTr\).

4. **The support-product lemma must give products, not only meets.**  
   Since \(\Omega\) need not be normal on the whole normal bidual, knowing only a meet formula is insufficient. The commuting product formula fixes this.

5. **No free-Haar or diffuseness assumption is available.**  
   The proof only uses trace-zero unitaries and works for finite-dimensional vertices, including order-two unitaries.

With these conventions and the allowed support-product lemma, the proof is complete.

self_declared_problem_solved:
Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

description: writeup (full problem): Let Gamma be a finite simplicial graph with at least three vertices, irreducible in the nontrivial-join sense, and let (M_v, tau_v) be separable tracial von Neumann algebras such that every M_v contains a unitary u_v with tau_v(u_v)=0. Then the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra M_Gamma is properly proximal.
full_text:
## Original Problem

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). Say that \(\Gamma\) is **irreducible** if it is not a nontrivial join of two nonempty induced subgraphs; equivalently, there is no partition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) into nonempty sets such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). For each \(v\in V\), let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra, and assume that \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) satisfying \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\ast_{\Gamma,v\in V}(M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph-product von Neumann algebra generated by the \(M_v\)’s with the usual commuting relations along edges of \(\Gamma\). Then \(M\) is properly proximal in the sense of Ding–Kunnawalkam Elayavalli–Peterson. This is exactly the original assertion, with \(\tau_v\) denoting the trace \(\varphi_v\).

## Proof

Let \(H=L^2(M,\tau)\), and let \(J\) be the modular conjugation. We write
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J,\qquad \rho(x)\widehat y=\widehat{yx},
\]
for right multiplication by \(x\in M\).

### 1. Graph-product subalgebras and DKEP boundary pieces

For an induced subset \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation, and let
\[
e_U:H\to L^2(M_U)
\]
be the corresponding Jones projection. We set \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), so \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\).

For such \(U\), let \(X_U\) denote the DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_U\): concretely, \(X_U\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(B(H)\) generated by the left-right \(M\)-conjugates of \(e_U\),
\[
xJyJ\, e_U\, Jy^*Jx^*,\qquad x,y\in M.
\]
Equivalently, \(X_U\) is the hereditary \(M\)-\(JMJ\)-invariant boundary piece generated by \(e_U\).

We use the following standard facts from the DKEP framework.

1. For every boundary piece \(X\), there is an operator system \(S_X(M)\subset B(H)\) containing \(M\) and \(X\), defined by the condition that commutators with the right \(M\)-action are \(X\)-compact. More precisely, if \(\mathcal K_X^{\infty,1}\) denotes the DKEP \(X\)-compact ideal, then
   \[
   S_X(M)=\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho(a)]\in \mathcal K_X^{\infty,1}\ \forall a\in M\}.
   \]
   It is enough to check this condition on an ultraweakly generating set of \(M\).

2. \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) if there is no \(M\)-central state \(\omega\) on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. Here \(M\)-central means
   \[
   \omega(xT)=\omega(Tx),\qquad x\in M,\ T\in S_X(M).
   \]

3. In the DKEP normal-bidual compactification there is, for each boundary piece \(X\), a support projection \(q_X\). It commutes with the left and right \(M\)-actions. If \(X\subset Y\), then \(q_X\le q_Y\). If \((h_\lambda)\) is a positive contractive approximate unit for a \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense hereditary subalgebra of \(X\), then \(h_\lambda\to q_X\) in the normal bidual, in fact \(\sigma\)-strongly after choosing the usual increasing approximate unit.

4. Let \(K=K(H)\) be the compact-operator boundary piece, with support projection \(q_K\). If \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then the DKEP obstruction theorem gives an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual compactification, normal on \(M\), such that
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0.
   \]

5. Compression principle: if \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), and \(\Omega\) is an absolute DKEP obstruction state as in (4), then
   \[
   \Omega(q_X)=1.
   \]
   Indeed, if \(r=1-q_X\) and \(\Omega(r)>0\), then
   \[
   T\mapsto \frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}
   \]
   defines an \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\), normal on \(M\), contradicting relative proper proximality. The point is that \(q_X\) commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\), and \(r\mathcal K_X^{\infty,1}r=0\).

We now prove the theorem.

### 2. The fixed-vertex link-relative ping-pong argument

Fix \(v\in V\). Let
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V:w\sim v\},
\]
\[
\operatorname{Star}(v)=L\cup\{v\},
\]
and set
\[
B=M_L,\qquad A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) commutes exactly with the vertices in \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\ \bar\otimes\ B,
\]
and the graph-product normal form gives the tracial amalgamated free-product decomposition
\[
M=A\ast_B C.
\]

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n,\ \epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n}.
\]
Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus all reduced AFP summands whose first letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly. Put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections and
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

#### 2.1. The sector projections lie in \(S_{X_B}(M)\)

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\overline D\) denote the other algebra. For \(x\in D^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_D)\), right multiplication by \(x\) changes only the last AFP letter. Hence it does not change whether a reduced word begins with \(D\). Therefore
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\overline D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_{\overline D},\rho(x)]
=
[e_B,\rho(x)]
=
e_B\rho(x)-\rho(x)e_B.
\]
For \(b\in B\), right multiplication by \(b\) preserves every AFP sector, so the commutators vanish. Thus for arbitrary \(x\in D\), after writing \(x=E_B(x)+x^\circ\), the commutators of \(R_A\) and \(R_C\) with \(\rho(x)\) are either zero or of the form
\[
e_B\rho(x^\circ)-\rho(x^\circ)e_B.
\]
Both terms factor through \(L^2(B)\), so they are \(X_B\)-compact. Since the vertex algebras generate \(M\), the DKEP generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
As \(e_B\in X_B\subset S_{X_B}(M)\), we also have
\[
P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]

#### 2.2. Construction of the ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\). Since \(A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B\), define
\[
a=u_v\otimes 1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1=0.
\]

We now construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1_B.
\]

Because \(\Gamma\) is irreducible, \(v\) has at least one non-neighbor. Otherwise \(\Gamma\) would be the nontrivial join
\[
\{v\}*\Gamma_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]

Let
\[
N(v)=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
be the set of non-neighbors of \(v\).

If \(|N(v)|\ge 2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and take trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\). Put
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}\in C.
\]
Then \(E_B(c_i)=0\), because \(w_i\notin L\), and
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1.
\]
For \(i\ne j\), the word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so it is orthogonal to \(L^2(B)\). Hence
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=0.
\]

Now suppose \(|N(v)|=1\), say \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Since \(|V|\ge 3\), the link \(L\) is nonempty. If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then \(\Gamma\) would be the join of the induced subgraphs on \(\{v,w\}\) and on \(L\), contradicting irreducibility. Hence there exists \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\).

Choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\subset B\). Define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Also
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
The diagonal relations are clear:
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1.
\]
For the cross term,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced; the two \(w\)-syllables cannot be shuffled together and cancelled. Thus the word still contains the vertex \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(u_w^*u_z u_w)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\). Therefore in all cases
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,\qquad E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1_B.
\]

#### 2.3. Projection ping-pong

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]
If \(d\in C\) is unitary and \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\). Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B)\), and multiplying a reduced AFP word beginning with \(A\) on the left by the centered \(C\)-letter \(d\) produces a reduced word beginning with \(C\). Hence
\[
dR_Ad^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(d=c_1,c_2\), we get
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

Moreover, the two ranges are orthogonal. For \(\xi,\eta\in H_A^+\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,c_1^*c_2\eta\rangle.
\]
Since \(c_1^*c_2\in C\) and \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\), the same argument gives
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_CH.
\]
But \(P_CH\perp H_A^+\), so the inner product vanishes. Therefore
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A\) and \(E_B(a)=0\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_AH\). Hence
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\]

#### 2.4. Contradiction for an \(M\)-central state

Suppose \(\omega\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries, centrality gives
\[
\omega(uTu^*)=\omega(T)
\]
for \(u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}\) and \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\).

From
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C,
\]
we obtain
\[
2\omega(R_A)\le \omega(P_C).
\]
From
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A,
\]
we obtain
\[
\omega(R_C)\le \omega(P_A).
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\omega(e_B),\qquad \beta=\omega(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\omega(P_C).
\]
Then \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge 0\) and
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
R_A=e_B+P_A,\qquad R_C=e_B+P_C,
\]
so
\[
\omega(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \omega(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
The two inequalities become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Thus
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
Since \(\alpha,\beta\ge 0\), we get \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le \beta\) gives \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). In particular, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_B=X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Since \(v\) was arbitrary, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to every link boundary piece.

### 3. The support-product lemma

We next prove the finite link-cover support formula.

For \(U\subset V\), write
\[
q_U:=q_{X_U}
\]
for the DKEP support projection of \(X_U\).

#### Lemma 3.1 — finite propagation

Let \(U,W\subset V\) be induced vertex sets, and put
\[
I=U\cap W.
\]
Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra. For \(a,b\in\mathcal A\), there exist finitely many \(\alpha_k,\beta_k,\gamma_k,\delta_k\in\mathcal A\) such that
\[
e_U L_aR_b e_W
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
L_{\alpha_k}R_{\beta_k}\, e_I\, L_{\gamma_k}R_{\delta_k}e_W,
\]
where \(L_aR_b\widehat z=\widehat{azb}\).

Equivalently, for every \(z\in\mathcal A\cap M_W\),
\[
E_U(azb)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n
\alpha_k\,E_I(\gamma_k z\delta_k)\,\beta_k.
\]

Proof. By linearity, it is enough to treat \(a,b,z\) as reduced graph-product words. Expand \(azb\) using the graph-product Wick reduction rules: commuting syllables are shuffled past one another, adjacent syllables from the same vertex algebra are multiplied, and each such product is decomposed into its scalar part plus its centered part.

Since \(a\) and \(b\) are fixed finite words, only finitely many syllables of \(z\) can participate in cancellations with \(a\) or \(b\). If, after all possible reductions, a syllable of \(z\) with vertex in \(W\setminus U\) remains, then the resulting word contains a vertex outside \(U\) and is killed by \(E_U\). Thus every term surviving under \(E_U\) has the following form: finitely many boundary syllables of \(z\) cancel against syllables of \(a\) and \(b\), and the remaining middle part of \(z\) has all vertices in \(I=U\cap W\).

There are only finitely many possible cancellation and shuffling patterns, depending only on \(a\) and \(b\). Each such pattern is represented by a map
\[
z\mapsto \alpha\,E_I(\gamma z\delta)\,\beta
\]
with fixed \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma,\delta\in\mathcal A\). Summing over the finitely many patterns gives the asserted formula. ∎

#### Lemma 3.2 — triple products are forced through the intersection

Let \(U,W\subset V\) and \(I=U\cap W\). Choose a countable set \(\mathcal U_0\) of algebraic unitaries whose linear span is \(L^2\)-dense in \(M\) and whose von Neumann algebra is \(M\). For \(r,s\in\mathcal U_0\), set
\[
p_{r,s}^U=L_rR_s e_U R_s^*L_r^*.
\]
Let \(\mathcal P_U^0\) be the nonunital algebraic \(*\)-algebra spanned by finite products of the projections \(p_{r,s}^U\) of positive length. Then
\[
\mathcal P_U^0\,\mathcal P_W^0\,\mathcal P_U^0
\subset \mathcal K_{X_I}^{\infty,1}.
\]

Proof. A monomial in
\[
\mathcal P_U^0\,\mathcal P_W^0\,\mathcal P_U^0
\]
is a finite product of algebraic left-right multipliers and projections \(e_U,e_W\). It has the schematic form
\[
A_0 e_{S_1} A_1 e_{S_2}\cdots A_{m-1}e_{S_m}A_m,
\]
where each \(A_j\) is an algebraic left-right multiplier \(L_aR_b\), each \(S_j\in\{U,W\}\), and the sequence contains a block of \(U\)’s, then a block of \(W\)’s, then a block of \(U\)’s. In particular, both transitions \(U\to W\) and \(W\to U\) occur.

Apply Lemma 3.1 to every transition \(e_U A e_W\) and \(e_W A e_U\). Each such transition is replaced by a finite sum of terms factoring through \(e_I\). Since \(I\subset U\) and \(I\subset W\), the same finite-propagation argument, now with \(I\) in place of one of the sets, absorbs adjacent \(e_U\)- or \(e_W\)-blocks into \(e_I\)-blocks. Iterating through the finite word expresses the monomial as a finite sum of operators belonging to the \(M\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule generated by the hereditary corner \(e_IB(H)e_I\). This is precisely contained in the DKEP \(X_I\)-compact ideal \(\mathcal K_{X_I}^{\infty,1}\). ∎

The point of using \(\mathcal P_U^0\) is important: although \(X_U\) contains the whole large corner \(e_UB(H)e_U\), one does not claim \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\), which is false in general. Instead, the support \(q_U\) can be computed from approximate units built out of finite algebraic conjugates of \(e_U\), and those approximate units satisfy the triple-product containment above.

#### Lemma 3.3 — support product formula

For all induced \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_{U\cap W}.
\]

Proof. Let \(I=U\cap W\). For a finite set \(F\subset \mathcal U_0\times\mathcal U_0\), put
\[
d_F^U=\sum_{(r,s)\in F}p_{r,s}^U.
\]
For \(\varepsilon>0\), define
\[
h_{F,\varepsilon}^U=d_F^U(d_F^U+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The functions \(t\mapsto t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) vanish at \(0\), so \(h_{F,\varepsilon}^U\) lies in the hereditary algebra generated by the finite family \(\{p_{r,s}^U:(r,s)\in F\}\). As \(F\) increases and \(\varepsilon\downarrow 0\), the net \((h_{F,\varepsilon}^U)\) is a positive contractive approximate unit for a \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense hereditary subalgebra of \(X_U\). Hence, in the DKEP normal bidual,
\[
h_{F,\varepsilon}^U\longrightarrow q_U
\]
\(\sigma\)-strongly. We similarly choose \(h_{G,\delta}^W\to q_W\).

By Lemma 3.2 and norm approximation by polynomials with zero constant term,
\[
h_{F,\varepsilon}^U\, h_{G,\delta}^W\, h_{F,\varepsilon}^U
\in \mathcal K_{X_I}^{\infty,1}.
\]
Therefore, in the normal bidual,
\[
0\le h_{F,\varepsilon}^U\, h_{G,\delta}^W\, h_{F,\varepsilon}^U\le q_I.
\]
Taking first the \((G,\delta)\)-limit and then the \((F,\varepsilon)\)-limit gives
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I.
\]

Since \(I\subset U\) and \(I\subset W\), we have \(X_I\subset X_U\cap X_W\), hence
\[
q_I\le q_U,\qquad q_I\le q_W.
\]
Let \(p=q_U\), \(q=q_W\), and \(r=q_I\). From \(r\le p,q\) and \(pqp\le r\), we get
\[
p(q-r)p=0.
\]
Thus \((q-r)p=0\), so
\[
qp=rp=r.
\]
Taking adjoints gives
\[
pq=r.
\]
Therefore
\[
q_Uq_W=q_Wq_U=q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
∎

By induction, for finitely many induced subsets \(U_1,\dots,U_n\subset V\),
\[
q_{U_1}\cdots q_{U_n}
=
q_{U_1\cap\cdots\cap U_n},
\]
and the projections commute.

Finally, for \(U=\varnothing\), \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). For \(x,y\in M\),
\[
L_x e_\varnothing R_{y^*}
\]
is the rank-one operator \(\theta_{\widehat x,\widehat y}\). These rank-one operators span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(H)\). Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(H),
\]
and therefore
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\]

### 4. Excluding the absolute obstruction state

First, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. Indeed, suppose \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) is a central projection such that \(zM\) is amenable. Then amenability gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace \(\psi\) on \(B(zH)\). Fix any vertex \(v\), and let \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Define
\[
\omega(T)=\psi(zTz),\qquad T\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Then \(\omega\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
\omega(xT)=\psi((zxz)(zTz))=\psi((zTz)(zxz))=\omega(Tx),
\]
so \(\omega\) is \(M\)-central on \(S_{X_B}(M)\), contradicting the fixed-vertex result above. Thus \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

Assume now, toward a contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, the DKEP obstruction theorem gives an \(M\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual compactification, normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\]

For every vertex \(v\), we proved that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to
\[
X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]
By the compression principle,
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad\forall v\in V.
\]

The support-product lemma gives
\[
\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]
Because the graph has no loops, no vertex belongs to its own link. Hence
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Therefore
\[
\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
=
q_\varnothing
=
q_K.
\]

The projections \(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\) commute. Since \(\Omega\) is a state and \(\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1\) for every \(v\), it follows that
\[
\Omega\left(\prod_{v\in V} q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Thus
\[
\Omega(q_K)=1,
\]
contradicting \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

Hence \(M\) is properly proximal. ∎
