solution:
## Original Problem

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite loopless simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). For \(v\in V\), write
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}:(v,w)\in E\},
\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}.
\]
Assume \(\Gamma\) is irreducible in the standard nontrivial-join sense: there is no partition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) into two nonempty subsets such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). Let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be separable finite von Neumann algebras with faithful normal tracial states, and assume that each \(M_v\) contains a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
(M,\tau)=\star_{\Gamma}(M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra: it is generated by the \(M_v\)’s, adjacent vertex algebras commute, and \(\tau\) kills all nonempty reduced products of centered letters.

For \(U\subset V\), let \(M_U\subset M\) be the parabolic graph-product subalgebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\), with \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), and let \(e_U:L^2(M)\to L^2(M_U)\) be the Jones projection. Let \(X_U\subset B(L^2M)\) be the DKEP boundary piece associated with \(M_U\), i.e. the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by operators \(L_x\rho_y e_U\), where \(x,y\in M\), \(L_x\) is left multiplication, and \(\rho_y=Jy^*J\) is right multiplication by \(y\). For a boundary piece \(X\), let
\[
S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\}.
\]
The von Neumann algebra \(M\) is properly proximal in the DKEP sense if there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S(M):=S_{K(L^2M)}(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

The proof below establishes exactly this statement, under the standard nontrivial-join interpretation of irreducibility. Thus it is not a relaxation of the intended original theorem.

---

# Proof

We use the standard graph-product normal form, the star-link amalgamated free product decomposition, the relative graph-product conditional expectation formula, and the DKEP/DE normal-bidual formalism. These are stated explicitly before use.

## Standard external facts used

1. **Graph products and parabolic expectations.**  
   The graph product \(M=\star_\Gamma(M_v,\tau_v)\) contains the \(M_v\)’s, adjacent vertex algebras commute, and the linear span of \(1\) and reduced graph-product words is weakly dense in \(M\). For every \(U\subset V\), the subalgebra \(M_U\) is naturally embedded in \(M\), and there is a trace-preserving conditional expectation \(E_U:M\to M_U\) killing reduced words containing at least one vertex outside \(U\). Caspers–Fima also prove that for every \(v\),
   \[
   M\cong M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)}*_{M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}}M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
   \]
   ([webusers.imj-prg.fr](https://webusers.imj-prg.fr/~pierre.fima/Documents/GraphProducts.pdf))

2. **Relative graph-product expectation formula.**  
   If \(V_1,V_2\subset V\), and \(x,\widetilde x\) are graph-reduced relative to \((V_1,V_2)\), then for \(y\in M_{V_1}\),
   \[
   E_{V_2}(\widetilde x^{\,*}yx)
   =
   \tau(\widetilde x^{\,*}x)E_U(y),
   \]
   where \(U\subset V_1\cap V_2\) is the set of vertices adjacent to all letters in the middle relative word; if the relative words are not equivalent, the scalar is \(0\). This is Lemma 3.17 of the graph-product structure paper. ([ems.press](https://ems.press/content/serial-article-files/51743))

3. **DKEP boundary pieces and \(S_X(M)\).**  
   DKEP define \(M\)-boundary pieces as hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebras \(X\subset B(L^2M)\) whose multiplier algebra contains weakly dense copies of \(M\) and \(JMJ\). For a subalgebra \(B\subset M\), the boundary piece \(X_B\) is the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(xJyJe_B\). They define \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\), prove that it agrees with the \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closure of \(K_X(M)\), and prove it is a self-adjoint strong \(M\)-\(M\) and \(JMJ\)-\(JMJ\) bimodule. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

4. **Proper proximality in the operator-system sense.**  
   DKEP define
   \[
   S_X(M)=\{T\in B(L^2M):[T,JxJ]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\ \forall x\in M\},
   \]
   and \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) iff there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal. For \(X=K(L^2M)\), this is ordinary proper proximality. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

5. **Normal-bidual support formalism.**  
   In the DKEP/DE normal-bidual von Neumann algebra, each compact corner \((K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\) has a support projection \(q_X\) commuting with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\). The normal-bidual characterization says that relative proper proximality can be tested by absence of central states on the corresponding normal-bidual compactification. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.00517))

6. **Approximate-unit convergence and Jones-projection commutation.**  
   DE prove that if \((h_i)\) is an approximate unit for a dense compact-corner subalgebra satisfying the normal-bidual commutation hypotheses, then \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h_i)\to q_X\) weak-*; they also prove that the normal support projection commutes with the image of every Jones projection \(e_N\). ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2211.05298))

7. **DKEP compact-corner obstruction.**  
   We use the following standard consequence of DKEP/DE: if \(M\) is separable tracial, has no amenable direct summand, and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state
   \[
   \Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
   \]
   on the absolute normal-bidual compactification, normal on \(M\), such that
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0,
   \]
   where \(q_K\) is the support projection of the compact boundary piece \(K(L^2M)\). The condition \(\Omega(q_K)=0\) is precisely the exclusion of the compact-corner/weak-compactness obstruction; if an obstruction state had positive compact-corner mass, DKEP’s weak-compactness/amenability alternative would produce an amenable direct summand.

---

## 1. Graph consequences of irreducibility

Irreducible means that \(\Gamma\) is not a nontrivial join: there is no partition
\[
V=V_1\sqcup V_2,\qquad V_1,V_2\ne\varnothing,
\]
with all cross edges present.

### 1.1. \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\neq V\)

Fix \(v\in V\). If \(\operatorname{Star}(v)=V\), then \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V\setminus\{v\}\). Since \(|V|\ge3\), both \(\{v\}\) and \(V\setminus\{v\}\) are nonempty, and
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup (V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join decomposition. Thus
\[
\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V.
\]

Therefore
\[
N(v):=V\setminus \operatorname{Star}(v)
\]
is nonempty for every \(v\).

### 1.2. Unique non-neighbor lemma

Suppose \(N(v)=\{w\}\). Then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup \operatorname{Link}(v).
\]
Since \(|V|\ge3\), \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\ne\varnothing\). If \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\), then every vertex of \(\{v,w\}\) would be adjacent to every vertex of \(\operatorname{Link}(v)\): this is clear for \(v\) by definition of the link, and true for \(w\) by assumption. Hence
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup\operatorname{Link}(v)
\]
would be a nontrivial join, contradiction. Thus:

> If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), there exists \(z\in\operatorname{Link}(v)\) such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\).

---

## 2. Fixed-vertex link-relative proper proximality

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad B=M_L,
\]
\[
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
By the star-link graph-product decomposition,
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]
Also
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes \operatorname{id}_B.
\]

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), and write \(\rho_x=Jx^*J\) for right multiplication by \(x\).

### 2.1. AFP first-letter sector projections

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),\qquad K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The AFP Hilbert space decomposition is
\[
L^2(M)=L^2(B)\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus all reduced AFP sectors whose first letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly. Put
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We claim
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and let \(\bar D\) denote the other algebra. Write
\[
D^\circ=\ker(E_B|_D).
\]
Right multiplication by \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. If \(d\in D^\circ\), right multiplication by \(d\) changes only the last AFP letter. Thus \(R_DH\) and its orthogonal complement are invariant under \(\rho(d)\), so
\[
[R_D,\rho(d)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
we get
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(d)]=[e_B,\rho(d)].
\]
But \(e_B\in X_B\), and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers on \(X_B\), so
\[
[e_B,\rho(d)]\in X_B\subset K_{X_B}^{\infty,1}(M).
\]
For arbitrary \(d\in D\), decompose \(d=E_B(d)+d^\circ\). The \(B\)-part preserves sectors, and the centered part is covered above.

Because \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), the DKEP strong-bimodule generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_B}(M).
\]
Since \(e_B\in S_{X_B}(M)\), also
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_B}(M)\).

### 2.2. Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\). Under
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\]
define
\[
a=u_v\otimes1_B.
\]
Then
\[
E_B(a)=\tau_v(u_v)1_B=0,
\]
so
\[
a\in A^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_A).
\]

We next construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C^\circ:=\ker(E_B|_C)\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

#### Case 1: \(|N(v)|\ge2\)

Choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\), and choose trace-zero unitaries \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\). Set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Each \(w_i\notin L\), so \(E_B(c_i)=0\). Also \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). For \(i\ne j\), the word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(L\), hence its \(B\)-expectation is \(0\). Thus
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

#### Case 2: \(N(v)=\{w\}\)

By the unique non-neighbor lemma, choose
\[
z\in L
\]
such that \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\). Pick trace-zero unitaries \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\subset B\). Define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Moreover,
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_2)=E_B(u_z u_w)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1.
\]
For the cross-term,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced: the two \(w\)-letters cannot be shuffled together and canceled. This remains true even if \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\). The word contains \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Taking adjoints gives \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\). Hence again
\[
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\]

### 2.3. Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]

If \(d\in C^\circ\), then left multiplication by \(d\) maps \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\). Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset K_C\), and if a reduced AFP word begins with an \(A\)-letter, then prepending the centered \(C\)-letter \(d\) gives a reduced word beginning with \(C\). Therefore
\[
dR_Ad^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(c_1,c_2\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

The two ranges are orthogonal. For \(\xi,\eta\in R_AH\),
\[
\langle c_1\xi,c_2\eta\rangle
=
\langle \xi,c_1^*c_2\eta\rangle.
\]
Since \(c_1^*c_2\in C^\circ\), left multiplication by \(c_1^*c_2\) maps \(R_AH\) into \(P_CH\), which is orthogonal to \(R_AH=L^2(B)\oplus P_AH\). Hence
\[
c_1R_AH\perp c_2R_AH.
\]
Thus
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\tag{2.1}
\]

Similarly, because \(a\in A^\circ\),
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{2.2}
\]

### 2.4. No \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\)

Suppose \(\phi\) is an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_B}(M)\). Since \(S_{X_B}(M)\) is an \(M\)-bimodule, centrality implies
\[
\phi(uTu^*)=\phi(T)
\]
for every unitary \(u\in M\) and \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\).

Applying \(\phi\) to (2.1),
\[
2\phi(R_A)\le \phi(P_C).
\tag{2.3}
\]
Applying \(\phi\) to (2.2),
\[
\phi(R_C)\le \phi(P_A).
\tag{2.4}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad \beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad \gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Since \(e_B,P_A,P_C\) are orthogonal projections summing to \(1\),
\[
\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0,\qquad \alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad \phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (2.3) and (2.4) become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le \gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le \beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge \alpha+\gamma\ge \alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence
\[
0\ge 3\alpha+\beta.
\]
So \(\alpha=\beta=0\), and then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state. In particular, there is no \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\), so
\[
M \text{ is properly proximal relative to } X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}.
\]
Since \(v\in V\) was arbitrary, \(M\) is properly proximal relative to every link boundary piece.

---

## 3. No amenable direct summand and the DKEP obstruction setup

First note that \(M\) is separable: choose countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subsets of each \(M_v\); finite reduced words in those countable sets span a countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subspace.

### 3.1. No amenable direct summand

We do not use a graph-product factor theorem. We prove directly that \(M\) has no nonzero amenable direct summand.

Suppose, toward contradiction, that \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) and \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
p=L_z
\]
be the projection of \(L^2(M)\) onto \(L^2(zM)\). Equip \(zM\) with the normalized trace
\[
\tau_z(zx)=\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)}.
\]
Since \(zM\) is finite amenable, there is a \(zM\)-central hypertrace
\[
\psi:B(L^2(zM))\to\mathbb C
\]
extending \(\tau_z\).

Fix any vertex \(v\), and put \(B=M_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). Define, for \(T\in S_{X_B}(M)\),
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi(pTp|_{pL^2(M)}).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
pL_xp=L_{zx}|_{pL^2(M)}
\]
is left multiplication by an element of \(zM\). Since \(p=L_z\) commutes with \(L_x\), and since \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central,
\[
\Phi(L_xT)
=
\psi((pL_xp)(pTp))
=
\psi((pTp)(pL_xp))
=
\Phi(TL_x).
\]
Thus \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central.

Moreover,
\[
\Phi(L_x)
=
\psi(pL_xp)
=
\tau_z(zx)
=
\frac{\tau(zx)}{\tau(z)},
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal. This contradicts Section 2, which showed that \(S_{X_B}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state at all.

Therefore \(M\) has no nonzero amenable direct summand.

### 3.2. Normal-bidual notation and obstruction theorem

Let
\[
\mathcal B=(B(L^2M)^\sharp_J)^*
\]
denote the DKEP/DE normal-bidual von Neumann algebra. For a boundary piece \(X\), let
\[
q_X\in\mathcal B
\]
be the support projection, i.e. the identity of the compact corner
\[
(K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*\subset \mathcal B.
\]
For \(U\subset V\), write
\[
q_U:=q_{X_U}.
\]
Let \(q_K\) denote the support projection for the compact boundary piece \(K(L^2M)\).

We will use the DKEP compact-corner obstruction in the following form:

> If a separable tracial von Neumann algebra \(N\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(N\)-central state \(\Omega\) on the absolute normal-bidual compactification \(\widetilde S(N)\), normal on \(N\), such that \(\Omega(q_K)=0\).

The phrase \(\Omega(q_K)=0\) is where weak compactness is excluded. A central state with nonzero compact-corner mass yields the weak-compactness/amenability obstruction; because we have already ruled out amenable direct summands, the obstruction state may be taken to vanish on \(q_K\).

---

## 4. The support-sandwich lemma

We prove the key support estimate:
\[
\boxed{q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}}
\tag{4.1}
\]
for all \(U,W\subset V\).

Let
\[
I=U\cap W.
\]

### 4.1. Algebraic cores and approximate units

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by \(1\) and reduced Wick words. For \(S\subset V\), let
\[
\mathcal A_S=\mathcal A\cap M_S.
\]
Let
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_a\rho_b:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\qquad
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_a\rho_b:a,b\in\mathcal A_S\}.
\]

For \(S\subset V\), define
\[
\mathscr S_S=\{L_a\rho_b e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
Let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra of \(B(L^2M)\) generated by \(\mathscr S_S\).

The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in \(K_{X_S}^{\infty,1}(M)\). Indeed, by Kaplansky density, if \(x,y\in M\), we can approximate them in \(\|\cdot\|_2\) by uniformly bounded \(a,b\in\mathcal A\). For \(\|m\|_\infty\le1\),
\[
\|(L_x\rho_y e_S-L_a\rho_b e_S)\widehat m\|_2
\le
\|y\|_\infty\|x-a\|_2+\|a\|_\infty\|y-b\|_2.
\]
The same estimate holds for adjoints. Hence hereditary products built from \(L_x\rho_y e_S\) are \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-approximated by algebraic ones. Also \(e_S\in\mathscr S_S\), so the large corner \(e_SB(L^2M)e_S\) is included in \(D_S\).

For finite \(F\subset\mathscr S_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), define
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]
The net \((h^S_{F,\varepsilon})\), ordered by inclusion of \(F\) and by \(\varepsilon\downarrow0\), is a positive contractive approximate unit for \(D_S\). Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) lies in
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_S).
\]

Let \(\iota:B(H)\to B(H)^{**}\) be the universal representation and let
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(T)=p_{\mathrm{nor}}\iota(T)p_{\mathrm{nor}}
\]
be the normal-bidual map. This map is not globally multiplicative. However, DE prove that \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with the images of \(M\), \(JMJ\), and every \(e_N\). Hence \(p_{\mathrm{nor}}\) commutes with
\[
\iota\big(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\big).
\]
Therefore \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is a \(*\)-homomorphism on
\[
C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W).
\tag{4.2}
\]

By the DE approximate-unit lemma,
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^S_{F,\varepsilon})
\longrightarrow q_S
\]
weak-* in \(\mathcal B\).

### 4.2. Finite propagation

We need the following finite-propagation statement.

> For all \(S,T\subset V\),
> \[
> e_S\mathcal C e_T
> \subset
> \operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}.
> \tag{4.3}
> \]

It suffices to prove this for \(L_a\rho_b\), where \(a,b\in\mathcal A\) are reduced words. Using the relative graph-product normal form, write
\[
a=a_Sa_0a_T,
\qquad
b=b_Tb_0b_S,
\]
where \(a_S,b_S\in\mathcal A_S\), \(a_T,b_T\in\mathcal A_T\), and the middle words \(a_0,b_0\) are reduced relative to \((S,T)\) in the appropriate left/right sense.

For \(z\in\mathcal A_T\),
\[
e_SL_a\rho_b e_T\widehat z
=
\widehat{E_S(azb)}
=
\widehat{a_S\,E_S(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0)\,b_S}.
\]
Apply the relative expectation formula with \(V_1=T\), \(V_2=S\), \(y=a_Tzb_T\in M_T\), \(\widetilde x^*=a_0\), and \(x=b_0\). If the middle relative words are not equivalent, the expectation is \(0\). If they are equivalent, then
\[
E_S(a_0(a_Tzb_T)b_0)
=
\tau(a_0b_0)E_J(a_Tzb_T),
\]
where \(J\subset S\cap T\) consists of vertices adjacent to all letters of the middle relative word.

Thus
\[
e_SL_a\rho_b e_T
=
\tau(a_0b_0)\,
L_{a_S}\rho_{b_S}\,e_J\,L_{a_T}\rho_{b_T}.
\]
This belongs to \(\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T\), with \(J\subset S\cap T\). Linearity gives (4.3).

### 4.3. Triple products land in \(X_I\)

Define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}
\{A_0e_SA_1e_S\cdots e_SA_n:
n\ge1,\ A_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\) lies in the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\). Indeed,
\[
ss^*\in \mathcal P_S
\]
for \(s\in\mathscr S_S\), hence \(A_{F,S}\in\mathcal P_S\). Since \(\mathcal P_S\) is an algebra and the function \(t\mapsto t(t+\varepsilon)^{-1}\) is uniformly approximable on bounded intervals by polynomials with zero constant term,
\[
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}\in\overline{\mathcal P_S}^{\|\cdot\|}.
\]

Now define the algebraic \(I\)-corner class
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}
\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\),
\[
e_JTe_K\in e_IB(H)e_I\subset X_I.
\]
Because \(M\) and \(JMJ\) multiply \(X_I\),
\[
\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\tag{4.4}
\]

We claim that for \(S=U\) or \(S=W\),
\[
\mathcal P_S\mathcal Z_I\subset\mathcal Z_I,
\qquad
\mathcal Z_I\mathcal P_S\subset\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{4.5}
\]
Indeed, if \(J\subset I\subset S\), then finite propagation gives
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_J
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_L\mathcal C:L\subset J\},
\]
and similarly
\[
e_J\mathcal C e_S
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_L\mathcal C:L\subset J\}.
\]
Multiplying a generator \(Ae_JTe_KB\) of \(\mathcal Z_I\) by one \(S\)-block keeps all intermediate Jones projections supported on subsets of \(I\). Iterating along a word in \(\mathcal P_S\) proves (4.5).

Next, for \(A,B\in\mathcal C\) and \(T\in B(H)\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in\mathcal Z_I.
\tag{4.6}
\]
By finite propagation,
\[
e_UAe_W=\sum_r A_r e_{J_r}B_r,
\qquad
e_WBe_U=\sum_s C_s e_{K_s}D_s,
\]
with \(J_r,K_s\subset U\cap W=I\). Therefore
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U
=
\sum_{r,s}
A_r e_{J_r}(B_rTC_s)e_{K_s}D_s
\in \mathcal Z_I.
\]

Now take
\[
P\in\mathcal P_U,\qquad Q\in\mathcal P_W,\qquad R\in\mathcal P_U.
\]
In the product \(PQR\), isolate the last \(e_U\) in \(P\), the first and last \(e_W\) in \(Q\), and the first \(e_U\) in \(R\). The middle part has the form covered by (4.6), hence lies in \(\mathcal Z_I\). The remaining prefix and suffix preserve \(\mathcal Z_I\) by (4.5). Thus
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U
\subset
\mathcal Z_I
\subset
X_I.
\tag{4.7}
\]

Since \(h^U_\lambda\in\overline{\mathcal P_U}\) and \(h^W_\mu\in\overline{\mathcal P_W}\), (4.7) gives
\[
h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I
\tag{4.8}
\]
for all algebraic approximate-unit indices \(\lambda,\mu\).

Importantly, we have not used or claimed \(X_UX_W\subset X_I\).

### 4.4. Weak-* order limit

Set
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda),
\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^W_\mu).
\]
Then
\[
a_\lambda\to q_U,
\qquad
b_\mu\to q_W
\]
weak-*.

By restricted multiplicativity (4.2),
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]
By (4.8), the operator inside belongs to \(X_I\subset K_{X_I}(M)\), and it is a positive contraction. Therefore
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{4.9}
\]

Fix \(\lambda\) and let \(\mu\to\infty\). Separate weak-* continuity of multiplication in \(\mathcal B\) gives
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\tag{4.10}
\]
Now let \(\lambda\to\infty\). Since \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\) and \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) weak-*, we get strong convergence \(a_\lambda\to q_U\): for every normal positive functional \(\omega\),
\[
\omega((q_U-a_\lambda)^2)\le \omega(q_U-a_\lambda)\to0.
\]
Hence
\[
a_\lambda q_Wa_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
weak-*. The order interval \([0,q_I]\) is weak-* closed, so (4.10) yields
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\]
This proves the support-sandwich lemma.

---

## 5. Finite-cover contradiction

Assume, toward contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. By Section 3, \(M\) has no amenable direct summand. Hence the DKEP obstruction theorem gives an \(M\)-central state
\[
\Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
\]
normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{5.1}
\]

### 5.1. Relative proper proximality forces \(\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1\)

Let \(X=X_U\) be any boundary piece such that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\). We claim
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\]

Put
\[
r=1-q_U.
\]
If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define for \(T\in\widetilde S_X(M)\),
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
This is well-defined because \(q_U\) commutes with \(M\) and \(JMJ\), and for \(a\in JMJ\),
\[
[rTr,a]=r[T,a]r.
\]
Since \([T,a]\) lies in the \(q_U\)-supported compact corner, the compression by \(r=1-q_U\) kills it. Hence \(rTr\in\widetilde S(M)\).

The state \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central because \(r\) commutes with \(M\). Its restriction to \(M\) is normal: for \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le rxr\le x,
\]
so
\[
0\le \Phi(x)\le \frac{\Omega(x)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
Domination by a normal positive functional implies normality.

Thus \(\Phi\) would be an \(M\)-central state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\), normal on \(M\), contradicting relative proper proximality. Hence \(\Omega(r)=0\), so
\[
\Omega(q_U)=1.
\]

Applying this with \(U=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), using Section 2, gives
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1
\qquad
\forall v\in V.
\tag{5.2}
\]

### 5.2. Iterating the support sandwich

We now use only the one-sided inequality
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]

If a state is initially defined only on the operator system \(\widetilde S(M)\), extend it to a state on the ambient normal-bidual \(C^*\)-algebra. This extension preserves values on the projections \(q_U\).

Let \(\phi\) be such an extension. Suppose
\[
\phi(q_U)=\phi(q_W)=1.
\]
In the GNS representation \((\pi,\mathcal H_\phi,\xi_\phi)\),
\[
\pi(q_U)\xi_\phi=\xi_\phi.
\]
Therefore
\[
\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)=\phi(q_W)=1.
\]
Using the support sandwich,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Hence
\[
1=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)\le \phi(q_{U\cap W})\le1,
\]
so
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\]

Iterating over the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V,
\]
and using (5.2), we obtain
\[
\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\tag{5.3}
\]

Because \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex belongs to its own link. Therefore
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Thus (5.3) gives
\[
\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\tag{5.4}
\]

### 5.3. Identifying \(q_\varnothing\) with \(q_K\)

For \(U=\varnothing\),
\[
M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1,
\]
and \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat1\subset L^2(M)\).

For \(a,b\in M\),
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
\]
is the rank-one operator
\[
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}.
\]
Since \(M\widehat1\) is dense in \(L^2(M)\), the norm-closed span of these operators is \(K(L^2M)\). Therefore
\[
X_\varnothing=K(L^2M),
\]
and hence
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{5.5}
\]

Combining (5.4) and (5.5),
\[
\Omega(q_K)=1,
\]
contradicting (5.1).

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.

full_text:
## Original Problem

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following precise self-contained statement. Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite loopless simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). We use the standard nontrivial-join interpretation of irreducibility: there is no partition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) into two nonempty subsets such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). For each \(v\in V\), let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra containing a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
M=\star_{\Gamma}(M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra: it is generated by trace-preserving copies of the \(M_v\)’s, adjacent vertex algebras commute, and the trace kills centered graph-reduced words. Then \(M\) is properly proximal in the DKEP sense, equivalently there is no \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\) on the absolute small-at-infinity operator system \(S(M)=S_{K(L^2M)}(M)\). This is the original theorem, with the usual nontrivial-join convention for “irreducible”; it is not a relaxation.

---

# Proof

## 1. Graph-product and DKEP notation

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), let \(J\) be the modular conjugation for the trace, and write
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J
\]
for right multiplication by \(x\in M\).

For \(U\subset V\), the **parabolic subalgebra** \(M_U\subset M\) is the von Neumann algebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\). We set \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation; on reduced graph-product words it kills exactly those words containing a vertex outside \(U\). Let
\[
e_U:H\to L^2(M_U)
\]
be the Jones projection.

For \(v\in V\),
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}: \{v,w\}\in E\},
\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}.
\]

For a parabolic \(M_U\), define the DKEP boundary piece \(X_U\subset B(H)\) as the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by the \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-translates of the \(e_U\)-corner \(e_UB(H)e_U\). Thus \(e_U\in X_U\), and \(M\) and \(JMJ\) act by multipliers of \(X_U\). For a general DKEP boundary piece \(X\), let \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\) denote the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed \(X\)-compact bimodule.

The concrete relative small-at-infinity operator system is
\[
S_X(M)
=
\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho(x)]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\text{ for every }x\in M\}.
\]

The normal-bidual version is as follows. Let \(\mathfrak B=(B(H)^\sharp_J)^*\) be the DKEP normal-bidual von Neumann algebra containing normal copies of \(M\), \(JMJ\), and the relevant compact corners. For a boundary piece \(X\), let
\[
\mathfrak K_X=(K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*
\]
be the corresponding weak-* closed compact ideal/corner, and let \(q_X\) be its identity projection. Define
\[
\widetilde S_X(M)
=
\{T\in\mathfrak B:[T,y]\in\mathfrak K_X\text{ for every }y\in JMJ\}.
\]
For \(U\subset V\), write \(q_U=q_{X_U}\). For the compact boundary \(K=K(H)\), write \(q_K\).

We shall use the following standard DKEP/DE facts.

---

## 2. External DKEP/DE input

### Lemma 2.1 — Concrete relative proper proximality

For a separable tracial von Neumann algebra \(M\) and a DKEP boundary piece \(X\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) if and only if there is no \(M\)-central state \(\phi\) on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

Here \(M\)-central means
\[
\phi(xT)=\phi(Tx),\qquad x\in M,\ T\in S_X(M).
\]

Moreover, to check \(T\in S_X(M)\), it is enough to verify \([T,\rho(x)]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\) on any von Neumann algebra generating set for \(M\).

### Lemma 2.2 — Normal-bidual equivalence

For separable tracial \(M\), the previous condition is equivalent to the nonexistence of an \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\) on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). Thus concrete relative proper proximality and normal-bidual relative proper proximality agree.

### Lemma 2.3 — Supports, obstruction state, and compression

For every boundary piece \(X\):

1. \(q_X\) commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\).

2. There is a compression inclusion
   \[
   q_X^\perp\,\widetilde S_X(M)\,q_X^\perp
   \subset
   q_K^\perp\,\widetilde S(M),
   \]
   where \(\widetilde S(M)=\widetilde S_K(M)\).

3. If \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state
   \[
   \Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
   \]
   normal on \(M\), such that
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0.
   \]

4. If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), and \(\Omega\) is an obstruction state as in (3), then
   \[
   \Omega(q_X)=1.
   \]

Indeed, for (4), let \(r=1-q_X\). If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define, for \(T\in\widetilde S_X(M)\),
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
The compression inclusion makes this well-defined as a state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). Because \(r\) commutes with \(M\), \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x,
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by the normal positive functional \(\Omega|_M/\Omega(r)\), hence is normal. This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X\). Thus \(\Omega(r)=0\), so \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

### Lemma 2.4 — Parabolic support sandwich

For all \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
No commutativity of \(q_U\) and \(q_W\) is asserted.

#### Detailed proof sketch of Lemma 2.4

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by reduced words, and put
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_a\rho(b):a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
For \(S\subset V\), let \(\mathcal A_S=\mathcal A\cap M_S\) and
\[
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_a\rho(b):a,b\in\mathcal A_S\}.
\]

The first input is restricted multiplicativity of the normal-bidual embedding. If
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(T)=p_{\mathrm{nor}}\iota(T)p_{\mathrm{nor}},
\]
then \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is not globally multiplicative. However, DE’s commutation theorem gives
\[
p_{\mathrm{nor}}\in \iota(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W))',
\]
and therefore \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is a \(*\)-homomorphism on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\). This is the only multiplicativity used.

For \(S\subset V\), let
\[
\mathscr S_S=\{L_a\rho(b)e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\]
and let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathscr S_S\). The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in the \(S\)-parabolic compact piece. Hence positive contractive approximate units \(h^S_\lambda\in D_S\cap C^*(M,JMJ,e_S)\) satisfy
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^S_\lambda)\xrightarrow[\lambda]{{\rm uw}} q_S.
\]

One may choose these \(h^S_\lambda\)’s as follows. For finite \(F\subset\mathscr S_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), set
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]

The graph-product finite-propagation estimate is:
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}.
\tag{FP}
\]
It follows from the relative reduced-word conditional expectation formula. For one term \(L_a\rho(b)\), write relative normal forms of \(a\) and \(b\) with respect to \((S,T)\). The expectation \(E_S(a(\cdot)b)\) is zero unless the middle relative words match; in the matching case it factors through \(E_J\) for some \(J\subset S\cap T\). This yields (FP).

Let \(I=U\cap W\). Define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}\{A_0e_SA_1e_S\cdots e_SA_n:n\ge1,\ A_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_\lambda\) lies in the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\).

Define also
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\), one has \(\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I\).

Using (FP), multiplication on either side by a word from \(\mathcal P_U\) or \(\mathcal P_W\) preserves \(\mathcal Z_I\). Also, for \(A,B\in\mathcal C\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in\mathcal Z_I,
\]
because (FP) gives
\[
e_UAe_W\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_J\mathcal C:J\subset I\},
\]
and similarly for \(e_WBe_U\). Therefore
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U\subset \mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\]
Passing to norm limits gives
\[
h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I.
\]

Set
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda),
\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^W_\mu).
\]
Restricted multiplicativity gives
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]
Since the product lies in \(X_I\), we have
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Letting \(\mu\to\infty\) ultraweakly yields
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Finally \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) ultraweakly and \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\), hence \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly. Therefore
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
ultraweakly. Since the order interval \([0,q_I]\) is ultraweakly closed,
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\]

---

## 3. Fixed-vertex link-relative ping-pong

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad
B=M_L,\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes\operatorname{id}_B.
\]
The graph-product normal form gives the amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]

### 3.1 Graph combinatorics

Because \(\Gamma\) is irreducible, \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\). Otherwise
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup(V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join. Thus
\[
N(v):=V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne\varnothing.
\]

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(L\ne\varnothing\) because \(|V|\ge3\). Moreover, there exists \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Indeed, if \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join.

### 3.2 Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\), and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=0.
\]

We now construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\tag{3.1}
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\). Let \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\) be trace-zero unitaries and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). For \(i\ne j\), the word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so its \(B\)-expectation is \(0\).

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Let \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\) be trace-zero unitaries, and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Since \(u_z\in B\) and \(E_B(u_w)=0\),
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word of type \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced. It contains \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\), and \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). Thus (3.1) holds. This also covers the order-two case \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), since the noncommuting middle letter \(u_z\) prevents cancellation.

### 3.3 AFP first-letter projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The \(A*_B C\) decomposition gives
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the reduced summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly. Set
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We claim
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and write \(D^\circ=\ker E_B|_D\). If \(x\in D^\circ\), right multiplication by \(x\) affects only the last AFP letter. Hence it preserves the subspace \(R_DH\) and its orthogonal complement, so
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
where \(\bar D\) is the other algebra,
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)].
\]
The operators \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\) lie in \(K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M)\), because \(e_B=e_L\in X_L\) and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers of \(X_L\).

Right multiplication by elements of \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. Since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), the DKEP generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Because \(e_B\in X_L\subset S_{X_L}(M)\), also
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_L}(M)\).

### 3.4 Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]

If \(d\in C\) is unitary and \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\). Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B)\), and a reduced word beginning with an \(A\)-letter becomes a reduced word beginning with a \(C\)-letter. Therefore
\[
dR_Ad^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(d=c_1,c_2\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

Moreover, \(c_1^*c_2\in C\) and \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\). Thus
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_CH\perp H_A^+.
\]
Hence \(c_1H_A^+\perp c_2H_A^+\), and therefore
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\tag{3.2}
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A\) is unitary and \(E_B(a)=0\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_AH\). Thus
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{3.3}
\]

### 3.5 Contradiction for central states

Let \(\phi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\phi(uTu^*)=\phi(T),
\qquad
u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}.
\]

From (3.2),
\[
2\phi(R_A)
=
\phi(c_1R_Ac_1^*)+\phi(c_2R_Ac_2^*)
\le
\phi(P_C).
\tag{3.4}
\]
From (3.3),
\[
\phi(R_C)=\phi(aR_Ca^*)\le\phi(P_A).
\tag{3.5}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Then \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0\) and
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad
\phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (3.4) and (3.5) become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le\gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le\beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge\alpha+\gamma\ge\alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence \(0\ge3\alpha+\beta\), so \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). By Lemmas 2.1 and 2.2,
\[
M\text{ is properly proximal relative to }X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\]
for every \(v\in V\).

---

## 4. No amenable direct summand

We prove this directly from the ping-pong result.

Assume toward contradiction that \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) is a central projection and \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
H_z=zH\cong L^2(zM)
\]
with normalized trace \(\tau_z(x)=\tau(z)^{-1}\tau(x)\) on \(zM\). Amenability gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace
\[
\psi:B(H_z)\to\mathbb C
\]
extending \(\tau_z\).

Fix \(v\in V\), set \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), and define, for \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\),
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi\bigl(zTz|_{H_z}\bigr).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\psi((zx)(zTz))
=
\psi((zTz)(zx))
=
\Phi(Tx),
\]
because \(zx\in zM\) and \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central. Also, for \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(x)=\tau_z(zx),
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal.

This contradicts the previous section, which showed that \(S_{X_L}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state at all. Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

## 5. Final contradiction: absolute proper proximality

Because \(V\) is finite and each \(M_v\) is separable, the reduced-word span built from countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subsets of the \(M_v\)’s is countable and \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense in \(M\). Thus \(M\) is separable, so the DKEP/DE results apply.

Assume, for contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, Lemma 2.3 gives an \(M\)-central state
\[
\Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
\]
normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{5.1}
\]

For every \(v\in V\), we proved that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). By Lemma 2.3,
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1,
\qquad v\in V.
\tag{5.2}
\]

The products of the support projections live naturally in the ambient normal-bidual \(C^*\)-algebra. Extend \(\Omega\) to a state \(\overline\Omega\) on that ambient \(C^*\)-algebra.

We now use only the one-sided support sandwich. Let \(\phi\) be any state on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra, and suppose
\[
\phi(q_U)=\phi(q_W)=1.
\]
In the GNS representation \((\pi_\phi,H_\phi,\xi_\phi)\),
\[
\pi_\phi(q_U)\xi_\phi=\xi_\phi.
\]
Hence
\[
\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)=\phi(q_W)=1.
\]
By Lemma 2.4,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Therefore
\[
1=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)\le\phi(q_{U\cap W})\le1,
\]
so
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\tag{5.3}
\]

Apply this inductively to the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V.
\]
Using (5.2), (5.3) gives
\[
\overline\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Since \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex lies in its own link. Thus
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\tag{5.4}
\]

It remains to identify \(q_\varnothing\). Since \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), the projection \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). For \(a,b\in M\),
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
=
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}.
\]
Because \(M\widehat1\) is dense in \(L^2(M)\), these rank-one operators span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(H)\). Conversely, every left-right translate of \(e_\varnothing\) is compact. Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(H),
\]
and therefore
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{5.5}
\]

Combining (5.4), (5.5), and (5.1),
\[
1=\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\overline\Omega(q_K)=\Omega(q_K)=0,
\]
a contradiction.

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.

If $\\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\\GG_1,\\GG_2$ such that $ \\VV(\\GG_1)\\cup \\VV(\\GG_2)=\\VV(\\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\\in \\EE(\\GG) $ for all $v\\in\\VV(\\GG_1),u\\in \\VV(\\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.",

For each v∈V let (M_v,φ_v) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra containing a φ_v-trace-zero unitary. For the tracial graph product M=∗_Γ(M_v,φ_v), M is properly proximal in the DKEP sense.",

solution:
## Original Problem

Prove the following statement:

Let $\Gamma$ be a finite simplicial graph with $|\VV(\GG)|\geq 3$, and $(M_v,\varphi_v)$ be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra for each $v\in \VV$. If $\Gamma$ is irreducible (in the sense that there does not exists subgraphs $\GG_1,\GG_2$ such that $ \VV(\GG_1)\cup \VV(\GG_2)=\VV(\GG) $ and every $(v,u)\in \EE(\GG) $ for all $v\in\VV(\GG_1),u\in \VV(\GG_2)$), and each $M_v$ has a trace zero unitary, then the graph product von Neumann algebra $M$ is properly proximal.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following precise self-contained statement. Let \(\Gamma=(V,E)\) be a finite loopless simplicial graph with \(|V|\ge 3\). We use the standard nontrivial-join interpretation of irreducibility: there is no partition \(V=V_1\sqcup V_2\) into two nonempty subsets such that every vertex of \(V_1\) is adjacent to every vertex of \(V_2\). For each \(v\in V\), let \((M_v,\tau_v)\) be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra containing a unitary \(u_v\) with \(\tau_v(u_v)=0\). Let
\[
M=\star_{\Gamma}(M_v,\tau_v)
\]
be the tracial graph product von Neumann algebra: it is generated by trace-preserving copies of the \(M_v\)’s, adjacent vertex algebras commute, and the trace kills centered graph-reduced words. Then \(M\) is properly proximal in the DKEP sense, equivalently there is no \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\) on the absolute small-at-infinity operator system \(S(M)=S_{K(L^2M)}(M)\). This is the original theorem, with the usual nontrivial-join convention for “irreducible”; it is not a relaxation.

---

# Proof

## 1. Graph-product and DKEP notation

Let \(H=L^2(M)\), let \(J\) be the modular conjugation for the trace, and write
\[
\rho(x)=Jx^*J
\]
for right multiplication by \(x\in M\).

For \(U\subset V\), the **parabolic subalgebra** \(M_U\subset M\) is the von Neumann algebra generated by \(\{M_u:u\in U\}\). We set \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\). Let
\[
E_U:M\to M_U
\]
be the trace-preserving conditional expectation; on reduced graph-product words it kills exactly those words containing a vertex outside \(U\). Let
\[
e_U:H\to L^2(M_U)
\]
be the Jones projection.

For \(v\in V\),
\[
\operatorname{Link}(v)=\{w\in V\setminus\{v\}: \{v,w\}\in E\},
\qquad
\operatorname{Star}(v)=\operatorname{Link}(v)\cup\{v\}.
\]

For a parabolic \(M_U\), define the DKEP boundary piece \(X_U\subset B(H)\) as the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by the \(M\)- and \(JMJ\)-translates of the \(e_U\)-corner \(e_UB(H)e_U\). Thus \(e_U\in X_U\), and \(M\) and \(JMJ\) act by multipliers of \(X_U\). For a general DKEP boundary piece \(X\), let \(K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\) denote the DKEP \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-closed \(X\)-compact bimodule.

The concrete relative small-at-infinity operator system is
\[
S_X(M)
=
\{T\in B(H):[T,\rho(x)]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\text{ for every }x\in M\}.
\]

The normal-bidual version is as follows. Let \(\mathfrak B=(B(H)^\sharp_J)^*\) be the DKEP normal-bidual von Neumann algebra containing normal copies of \(M\), \(JMJ\), and the relevant compact corners. For a boundary piece \(X\), let
\[
\mathfrak K_X=(K_X(M)^\sharp_J)^*
\]
be the corresponding weak-* closed compact ideal/corner, and let \(q_X\) be its identity projection. Define
\[
\widetilde S_X(M)
=
\{T\in\mathfrak B:[T,y]\in\mathfrak K_X\text{ for every }y\in JMJ\}.
\]
For \(U\subset V\), write \(q_U=q_{X_U}\). For the compact boundary \(K=K(H)\), write \(q_K\).

We shall use the following standard DKEP/DE facts.

---

## 2. External DKEP/DE input

### Lemma 2.1 — Concrete relative proper proximality

For a separable tracial von Neumann algebra \(M\) and a DKEP boundary piece \(X\), \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\) if and only if there is no \(M\)-central state \(\phi\) on \(S_X(M)\) whose restriction to \(M\) is normal.

Here \(M\)-central means
\[
\phi(xT)=\phi(Tx),\qquad x\in M,\ T\in S_X(M).
\]

Moreover, to check \(T\in S_X(M)\), it is enough to verify \([T,\rho(x)]\in K_X^{\infty,1}(M)\) on any von Neumann algebra generating set for \(M\).

### Lemma 2.2 — Normal-bidual equivalence

For separable tracial \(M\), the previous condition is equivalent to the nonexistence of an \(M\)-central state normal on \(M\) on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). Thus concrete relative proper proximality and normal-bidual relative proper proximality agree.

### Lemma 2.3 — Supports, obstruction state, and compression

For every boundary piece \(X\):

1. \(q_X\) commutes with both \(M\) and \(JMJ\).

2. There is a compression inclusion
   \[
   q_X^\perp\,\widetilde S_X(M)\,q_X^\perp
   \subset
   q_K^\perp\,\widetilde S(M),
   \]
   where \(\widetilde S(M)=\widetilde S_K(M)\).

3. If \(M\) has no amenable direct summand and is not properly proximal, then there exists an \(M\)-central state
   \[
   \Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
   \]
   normal on \(M\), such that
   \[
   \Omega(q_K)=0.
   \]

4. If \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X\), and \(\Omega\) is an obstruction state as in (3), then
   \[
   \Omega(q_X)=1.
   \]

Indeed, for (4), let \(r=1-q_X\). If \(\Omega(r)>0\), define, for \(T\in\widetilde S_X(M)\),
\[
\Phi(T)=\frac{\Omega(rTr)}{\Omega(r)}.
\]
The compression inclusion makes this well-defined as a state on \(\widetilde S_X(M)\). Because \(r\) commutes with \(M\), \(\Phi\) is \(M\)-central. For \(x\in M_+\),
\[
0\le r x r\le x,
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is dominated by the normal positive functional \(\Omega|_M/\Omega(r)\), hence is normal. This contradicts relative proper proximality to \(X\). Thus \(\Omega(r)=0\), so \(\Omega(q_X)=1\).

### Lemma 2.4 — Parabolic support sandwich

For all \(U,W\subset V\),
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
No commutativity of \(q_U\) and \(q_W\) is asserted.

#### Detailed proof sketch of Lemma 2.4

Let \(\mathcal A\subset M\) be the algebraic graph-product \(*\)-algebra spanned by reduced words, and put
\[
\mathcal C=\operatorname{span}\{L_a\rho(b):a,b\in\mathcal A\}.
\]
For \(S\subset V\), let \(\mathcal A_S=\mathcal A\cap M_S\) and
\[
\mathcal C_S=\operatorname{span}\{L_a\rho(b):a,b\in\mathcal A_S\}.
\]

The first input is restricted multiplicativity of the normal-bidual embedding. If
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(T)=p_{\mathrm{nor}}\iota(T)p_{\mathrm{nor}},
\]
then \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is not globally multiplicative. However, DE’s commutation theorem gives
\[
p_{\mathrm{nor}}\in \iota(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W))',
\]
and therefore \(\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}\) is a \(*\)-homomorphism on \(C^*(M,JMJ,e_U,e_W)\). This is the only multiplicativity used.

For \(S\subset V\), let
\[
\mathscr S_S=\{L_a\rho(b)e_S:a,b\in\mathcal A\},
\]
and let \(D_S\) be the hereditary \(C^*\)-subalgebra generated by \(\mathscr S_S\). The algebraic core \(D_S\) is \(\|\cdot\|_{\infty,1}\)-dense in the \(S\)-parabolic compact piece. Hence positive contractive approximate units \(h^S_\lambda\in D_S\cap C^*(M,JMJ,e_S)\) satisfy
\[
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^S_\lambda)\xrightarrow[\lambda]{{\rm uw}} q_S.
\]

One may choose these \(h^S_\lambda\)’s as follows. For finite \(F\subset\mathscr S_S\) and \(\varepsilon>0\), set
\[
A_{F,S}=\sum_{s\in F}ss^*,
\qquad
h^S_{F,\varepsilon}=A_{F,S}(A_{F,S}+\varepsilon)^{-1}.
\]

The graph-product finite-propagation estimate is:
\[
e_S\mathcal C e_T
\subset
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C_S e_J\mathcal C_T:J\subset S\cap T\}.
\tag{FP}
\]
It follows from the relative reduced-word conditional expectation formula. For one term \(L_a\rho(b)\), write relative normal forms of \(a\) and \(b\) with respect to \((S,T)\). The expectation \(E_S(a(\cdot)b)\) is zero unless the middle relative words match; in the matching case it factors through \(E_J\) for some \(J\subset S\cap T\). This yields (FP).

Let \(I=U\cap W\). Define
\[
\mathcal P_S
=
\operatorname{span}\{A_0e_SA_1e_S\cdots e_SA_n:n\ge1,\ A_i\in\mathcal C\}.
\]
Each \(h^S_\lambda\) lies in the norm closure of \(\mathcal P_S\).

Define also
\[
\mathcal Z_I
=
\operatorname{span}\{A e_J T e_K B:
A,B\in\mathcal C,\ J,K\subset I,\ T\in B(H)\}.
\]
Since \(e_J,e_K\le e_I\), one has \(\mathcal Z_I\subset X_I\).

Using (FP), multiplication on either side by a word from \(\mathcal P_U\) or \(\mathcal P_W\) preserves \(\mathcal Z_I\). Also, for \(A,B\in\mathcal C\),
\[
e_UAe_W\,T\,e_WBe_U\in\mathcal Z_I,
\]
because (FP) gives
\[
e_UAe_W\in
\operatorname{span}\{\mathcal C e_J\mathcal C:J\subset I\},
\]
and similarly for \(e_WBe_U\). Therefore
\[
\mathcal P_U\mathcal P_W\mathcal P_U\subset \mathcal Z_I\subset X_I.
\]
Passing to norm limits gives
\[
h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda\in X_I.
\]

Set
\[
a_\lambda=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda),
\qquad
b_\mu=\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^W_\mu).
\]
Restricted multiplicativity gives
\[
a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda
=
\iota_{\mathrm{nor}}(h^U_\lambda h^W_\mu h^U_\lambda).
\]
Since the product lies in \(X_I\), we have
\[
0\le a_\lambda b_\mu a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Letting \(\mu\to\infty\) ultraweakly yields
\[
0\le a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\le q_I.
\]
Finally \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) ultraweakly and \(0\le a_\lambda\le q_U\), hence \(a_\lambda\to q_U\) strongly. Therefore
\[
a_\lambda q_W a_\lambda\to q_Uq_Wq_U
\]
ultraweakly. Since the order interval \([0,q_I]\) is ultraweakly closed,
\[
q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_I=q_{U\cap W}.
\]

---

## 3. Fixed-vertex link-relative ping-pong

Fix \(v\in V\). Put
\[
L=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad
B=M_L,\qquad
A=M_{\operatorname{Star}(v)},\qquad
C=M_{V\setminus\{v\}}.
\]
Since \(v\) is adjacent to every vertex in \(L\),
\[
A\cong M_v\bar\otimes B,
\qquad
E_B|_A=\tau_v\otimes\operatorname{id}_B.
\]
The graph-product normal form gives the amalgamated free product decomposition
\[
M=A*_B C.
\]

### 3.1 Graph combinatorics

Because \(\Gamma\) is irreducible, \(\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne V\). Otherwise
\[
V=\{v\}\sqcup(V\setminus\{v\})
\]
would be a nontrivial join. Thus
\[
N(v):=V\setminus\operatorname{Star}(v)\ne\varnothing.
\]

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), then \(L\ne\varnothing\) because \(|V|\ge3\). Moreover, there exists \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Indeed, if \(w\) were adjacent to every \(z\in L\), then
\[
V=\{v,w\}\sqcup L
\]
would be a nontrivial join.

### 3.2 Ping-pong unitaries

Choose a trace-zero unitary \(u_v\in M_v\), and set
\[
a=u_v\otimes1_B\in A.
\]
Then \(a\) is unitary and
\[
E_B(a)=0.
\]

We now construct unitaries \(c_1,c_2\in C\) satisfying
\[
E_B(c_i)=0,
\qquad
E_B(c_i^*c_j)=\delta_{ij}1.
\tag{3.1}
\]

If \(|N(v)|\ge2\), choose distinct \(w_1,w_2\in N(v)\). Let \(u_{w_i}\in M_{w_i}\) be trace-zero unitaries and set
\[
c_i=u_{w_i}.
\]
Then \(c_i\in C\), \(E_B(c_i)=0\), and \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). For \(i\ne j\), the word \(u_{w_i}^*u_{w_j}\) contains vertices outside \(L\), so its \(B\)-expectation is \(0\).

If \(N(v)=\{w\}\), choose \(z\in L\) not adjacent to \(w\). Let \(u_w\in M_w\) and \(u_z\in M_z\) be trace-zero unitaries, and define
\[
c_1=u_w,\qquad c_2=u_z u_w.
\]
Then \(c_1,c_2\in C\) are unitaries. Since \(u_z\in B\) and \(E_B(u_w)=0\),
\[
E_B(c_1)=0,\qquad E_B(c_2)=u_zE_B(u_w)=0.
\]
Also,
\[
c_1^*c_2=u_w^*u_z u_w.
\]
Because \(z\) is not adjacent to \(w\), the word of type \(w,z,w\) is graph-reduced. It contains \(w\notin L\), so
\[
E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0.
\]
Similarly \(E_B(c_2^*c_1)=0\), and \(E_B(c_i^*c_i)=1\). Thus (3.1) holds. This also covers the order-two case \(u_w=u_w^*=u_w^{-1}\), since the noncommuting middle letter \(u_z\) prevents cancellation.

### 3.3 AFP first-letter projections lie in \(S_{X_L}(M)\)

Let
\[
K_A=L^2(A)\ominus L^2(B),
\qquad
K_C=L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B).
\]
The \(A*_B C\) decomposition gives
\[
L^2(M)
=
L^2(B)
\oplus
\bigoplus_{\epsilon_1\ne\cdots\ne\epsilon_n}
K_{\epsilon_1}\otimes_B\cdots\otimes_B K_{\epsilon_n},
\]
where each \(\epsilon_i\in\{A,C\}\).

Let \(R_A\) be the projection onto \(L^2(B)\) plus the reduced summands whose first AFP letter is \(A\). Define \(R_C\) similarly. Set
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B.
\]
Then
\[
1=e_B+P_A+P_C.
\]

We claim
\[
R_A,R_C,P_A,P_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]

Let \(D\in\{A,C\}\), and write \(D^\circ=\ker E_B|_D\). If \(x\in D^\circ\), right multiplication by \(x\) affects only the last AFP letter. Hence it preserves the subspace \(R_DH\) and its orthogonal complement, so
\[
[R_D,\rho(x)]=0.
\]
Since
\[
R_{\bar D}=1-R_D+e_B,
\]
where \(\bar D\) is the other algebra,
\[
[R_{\bar D},\rho(x)]=[e_B,\rho(x)].
\]
The operators \(e_B\rho(x)\) and \(\rho(x)e_B\) lie in \(K_{X_L}^{\infty,1}(M)\), because \(e_B=e_L\in X_L\) and \(JMJ\) acts by multipliers of \(X_L\).

Right multiplication by elements of \(B\) preserves every AFP sector. Since \(A\) and \(C\) generate \(M\), the DKEP generator criterion gives
\[
R_A,R_C\in S_{X_L}(M).
\]
Because \(e_B\in X_L\subset S_{X_L}(M)\), also
\[
P_A=R_A-e_B,\qquad P_C=R_C-e_B
\]
belong to \(S_{X_L}(M)\).

### 3.4 Projection inequalities

Let
\[
H_A^+=R_AH,\qquad H_C^+=R_CH.
\]

If \(d\in C\) is unitary and \(E_B(d)=0\), then left multiplication by \(d\) sends \(H_A^+\) into \(P_CH\). Indeed, \(dL^2(B)\subset L^2(C)\ominus L^2(B)\), and a reduced word beginning with an \(A\)-letter becomes a reduced word beginning with a \(C\)-letter. Therefore
\[
dR_Ad^*\le P_C.
\]
Applying this to \(d=c_1,c_2\),
\[
c_iR_Ac_i^*\le P_C.
\]

Moreover, \(c_1^*c_2\in C\) and \(E_B(c_1^*c_2)=0\). Thus
\[
(c_1^*c_2)H_A^+\subset P_CH\perp H_A^+.
\]
Hence \(c_1H_A^+\perp c_2H_A^+\), and therefore
\[
c_1R_Ac_1^*+c_2R_Ac_2^*\le P_C.
\tag{3.2}
\]

Similarly, since \(a\in A\) is unitary and \(E_B(a)=0\), left multiplication by \(a\) sends \(H_C^+\) into \(P_AH\). Thus
\[
aR_Ca^*\le P_A.
\tag{3.3}
\]

### 3.5 Contradiction for central states

Let \(\phi\) be an \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). Since \(a,c_1,c_2\in M\) are unitaries,
\[
\phi(uTu^*)=\phi(T),
\qquad
u\in\{a,c_1,c_2\}.
\]

From (3.2),
\[
2\phi(R_A)
=
\phi(c_1R_Ac_1^*)+\phi(c_2R_Ac_2^*)
\le
\phi(P_C).
\tag{3.4}
\]
From (3.3),
\[
\phi(R_C)=\phi(aR_Ca^*)\le\phi(P_A).
\tag{3.5}
\]

Set
\[
\alpha=\phi(e_B),\qquad
\beta=\phi(P_A),\qquad
\gamma=\phi(P_C).
\]
Then \(\alpha,\beta,\gamma\ge0\) and
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]
Also
\[
\phi(R_A)=\alpha+\beta,\qquad
\phi(R_C)=\alpha+\gamma.
\]
Thus (3.4) and (3.5) become
\[
2(\alpha+\beta)\le\gamma,
\qquad
\alpha+\gamma\le\beta.
\]
Combining,
\[
\beta\ge\alpha+\gamma\ge\alpha+2(\alpha+\beta)=3\alpha+2\beta.
\]
Hence \(0\ge3\alpha+\beta\), so \(\alpha=\beta=0\). Then \(\alpha+\gamma\le\beta\) forces \(\gamma=0\), contradicting
\[
\alpha+\beta+\gamma=1.
\]

Therefore there is no \(M\)-central state on \(S_{X_L}(M)\). By Lemmas 2.1 and 2.2,
\[
M\text{ is properly proximal relative to }X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}
\]
for every \(v\in V\).

---

## 4. No amenable direct summand

We prove this directly from the ping-pong result.

Assume toward contradiction that \(0\ne z\in Z(M)\) is a central projection and \(zM\) is amenable. Let
\[
H_z=zH\cong L^2(zM)
\]
with normalized trace \(\tau_z(x)=\tau(z)^{-1}\tau(x)\) on \(zM\). Amenability gives a \(zM\)-central hypertrace
\[
\psi:B(H_z)\to\mathbb C
\]
extending \(\tau_z\).

Fix \(v\in V\), set \(L=\operatorname{Link}(v)\), and define, for \(T\in S_{X_L}(M)\),
\[
\Phi(T)=\psi\bigl(zTz|_{H_z}\bigr).
\]
Then \(\Phi\) is a state. For \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(xT)
=
\psi((zx)(zTz))
=
\psi((zTz)(zx))
=
\Phi(Tx),
\]
because \(zx\in zM\) and \(\psi\) is \(zM\)-central. Also, for \(x\in M\),
\[
\Phi(x)=\tau_z(zx),
\]
so \(\Phi|_M\) is normal.

This contradicts the previous section, which showed that \(S_{X_L}(M)\) admits no \(M\)-central state at all. Hence \(M\) has no amenable direct summand.

---

## 5. Final contradiction: absolute proper proximality

Because \(V\) is finite and each \(M_v\) is separable, the reduced-word span built from countable \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense subsets of the \(M_v\)’s is countable and \(\|\cdot\|_2\)-dense in \(M\). Thus \(M\) is separable, so the DKEP/DE results apply.

Assume, for contradiction, that \(M\) is not properly proximal. Since \(M\) has no amenable direct summand, Lemma 2.3 gives an \(M\)-central state
\[
\Omega:\widetilde S(M)\to\mathbb C
\]
normal on \(M\), such that
\[
\Omega(q_K)=0.
\tag{5.1}
\]

For every \(v\in V\), we proved that \(M\) is properly proximal relative to \(X_{\operatorname{Link}(v)}\). By Lemma 2.3,
\[
\Omega(q_{\operatorname{Link}(v)})=1,
\qquad v\in V.
\tag{5.2}
\]

The products of the support projections live naturally in the ambient normal-bidual \(C^*\)-algebra. Extend \(\Omega\) to a state \(\overline\Omega\) on that ambient \(C^*\)-algebra.

We now use only the one-sided support sandwich. Let \(\phi\) be any state on the ambient \(C^*\)-algebra, and suppose
\[
\phi(q_U)=\phi(q_W)=1.
\]
In the GNS representation \((\pi_\phi,H_\phi,\xi_\phi)\),
\[
\pi_\phi(q_U)\xi_\phi=\xi_\phi.
\]
Hence
\[
\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)=\phi(q_W)=1.
\]
By Lemma 2.4,
\[
0\le q_Uq_Wq_U\le q_{U\cap W}.
\]
Therefore
\[
1=\phi(q_Uq_Wq_U)\le\phi(q_{U\cap W})\le1,
\]
so
\[
\phi(q_{U\cap W})=1.
\tag{5.3}
\]

Apply this inductively to the finite family
\[
U_v=\operatorname{Link}(v),\qquad v\in V.
\]
Using (5.2), (5.3) gives
\[
\overline\Omega\left(q_{\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)}\right)=1.
\]
Since \(\Gamma\) is loopless, no vertex lies in its own link. Thus
\[
\bigcap_{v\in V}\operatorname{Link}(v)=\varnothing.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=1.
\tag{5.4}
\]

It remains to identify \(q_\varnothing\). Since \(M_\varnothing=\mathbb C1\), the projection \(e_\varnothing\) is the rank-one projection onto \(\mathbb C\widehat 1\). For \(a,b\in M\),
\[
L_a e_\varnothing L_b^*
=
\theta_{\widehat a,\widehat b}.
\]
Because \(M\widehat1\) is dense in \(L^2(M)\), these rank-one operators span a norm-dense subalgebra of \(K(H)\). Conversely, every left-right translate of \(e_\varnothing\) is compact. Hence
\[
X_\varnothing=K(H),
\]
and therefore
\[
q_\varnothing=q_K.
\tag{5.5}
\]

Combining (5.4), (5.5), and (5.1),
\[
1=\overline\Omega(q_\varnothing)=\overline\Omega(q_K)=\Omega(q_K)=0,
\]
a contradiction.

Therefore \(M\) is properly proximal.
